# Codex for App Development

## Overview
- **Date**: 2026-03-31 18:56:16
- **Conversation ID**: `69cc5f1d-8270-832f-b023-b3afce16400b`
- **Source**: `Chat-Export-28513-2026/conversations-005.json`

## Chat History

### User
> ok i have codex installed on my pc how can it help us get this app actually working and usable.

### Assistant
> I’m going to pin this to two things: what Codex can actually do right now, and where your repo is obviously stuck so we don’t turn “AI help” into more architecture cosplay.

### Assistant
> I already found the real bottleneck in your own blueprint: the “first working milestone” is still the spine, not the full dream app. So Codex should be aimed at booting the spine end-to-end, not freelancing new abstractions.

### Assistant
> Yes. **Codex can help a lot** — but only if you use it as an execution engine, not as another brainstorming buddy.
> 
> ## The blunt truth
> 
> Your app is probably **not failing because it needs more ideas**. It’s failing because the boot path, schema, auth, API wiring, and state contracts are not locked and verified end-to-end.
> 
> That lines up with your own blueprint: the open decisions still include locking the ingest schema, `python_local/file_pipeline`, local API endpoints, review state machine, and Postgres table definitions, and the first real milestone is the spine being alive rather than the full fantasy platform. Based on your [QiOS Master Blueprint](sandbox:/mnt/data/__QiOS_Master_Blueprint_v0.4.pdf).
> 
> ## What Codex is actually good for
> 
> Codex can **read, modify, and run code**. It works in local tools and as a cloud coding agent, and OpenAI’s current docs describe it as able to help with bug fixing, tests, refactors, code review, and drafting PRs. Cloud tasks run in a sandboxed environment and can work against GitHub-connected repos. OpenAI also says Codex is available across the web app, IDE extension, and coding workflows like Cursor and Windsurf, with cloud delegation supported from several clients. citeturn557818search0turn557818search1turn557818search7
> 
> So the move is not “Codex, build my empire.”
> The move is: **“Codex, make this repo boot, prove the critical flow, then harden it.”**
> 
> ## Where Codex should attack your app
> 
> ### 1. Repo reality check
> First task: make Codex tell you what is actually runnable today.
> 
> Give it this:
> 
> ```text
> Audit this repository and determine the true runnable path for the app.
> 
> Deliver:
> 1. Which app(s) are intended to run now
> 2. Exact startup commands
> 3. Missing env vars
> 4. Broken imports / missing modules / dead folders
> 5. Which parts are stubbed vs implemented
> 6. A minimal runbook to get the system booting locally
> 
> Do not redesign architecture.
> Do not create new abstractions unless required to make the current app run.
> Prefer the existing blueprint and existing folder structure.
> ```
> 
> That gets you out of hallucination-land fast.
> 
> ### 2. Boot the spine, not the whole kingdom
> Your blueprint already defines the minimal useful system: local intake → registration → extraction → embedding → pgvector → app search/view.
> 
> So the next Codex task should be:
> 
> ```text
> Make the minimum changes required so the spine works end-to-end.
> 
> Success criteria:
> - frontend loads
> - local API starts
> - Supabase connection succeeds
> - /status works
> - /queue works
> - /ingest accepts a test file
> - a file is registered in qiarchive
> - failure states are visible and retryable
> 
> Do not add new product features.
> Patch only what is necessary to satisfy the success criteria.
> Show every changed file and why it changed.
> ```
> 
> That is the right level of constraint. Not vague, not bloated.
> 
> ### 3. Fix the “can’t add users / nothing talks” problem
> This is exactly the kind of targeted task Codex is strong at.
> 
> Use:
> 
> ```text
> Trace the full user creation flow from UI to database.
> 
> I need:
> - where the add-user action starts in the frontend
> - which API/function it calls
> - which auth/provider path it depends on
> - where it fails
> - the exact patch to make it work
> 
> Also verify tenant membership / RBAC insertion and report any RLS or auth mismatch.
> Do not refactor unrelated code.
> ```
> 
> That forces Codex to follow the wire instead of vibing.
> 
> ### 4. Lock schema and migrations
> OpenAI’s docs explicitly position Codex for code changes, tests, and unfamiliar codebase understanding. That makes it useful for **migration drift cleanup**, not just app code. citeturn557818search0turn557818search3
> 
> Use:
> 
> ```text
> Compare the current codebase assumptions against the actual Supabase migrations and schema usage.
> 
> Deliver:
> - tables/functions referenced by code but missing in DB
> - DB objects that exist but are unused
> - mismatched column names/types
> - RLS/auth assumptions that will break app flows
> - one ordered patch plan to reconcile code to migrations
> 
> Do not invent new schemas unless absolutely required.
> ```
> 
> ### 5. Add smoke tests before more features
> Do not let Codex keep “fixing” stuff without proving it.
> 
> Use:
> 
> ```text
> Create a minimal smoke-test suite for the current app.
> 
> Must verify:
> - app boot
> - local API health
> - Supabase connectivity
> - auth session resolution
> - add-user flow
> - ingest flow
> - one retrieval/query path
> 
> Use the lightest testing approach already compatible with this repo.
> ```
> 
> That’s how you stop the repo from sliding back into decorative complexity.
> 
> ## How to use Codex without wasting time
> 
> Use **Ask mode first**, then **Code mode**. OpenAI’s docs describe that split directly: Ask mode for analysis and architecture understanding, Code mode for making actual repo changes. citeturn557818search0
> 
> Your operating rule should be:
> 
> 1. **Ask mode**: map the system, identify failure point, propose minimal patch.
> 2. **Code mode**: apply only the approved patch.
> 3. **Run tests / run boot commands**.
> 4. **Summarize changed files and remaining blockers**.
> 
> Do **not** tell Codex “finish the app.”
> Tell it one bounded ticket at a time.
> 
> ## The best way Codex helps you this week
> 
> Define “usable” like this:
> 
> - app loads
> - auth works
> - user creation works
> - tenant membership resolves
> - local API works
> - test file ingests
> - archive record exists
> - one search/review screen shows real data
> 
> That is usable.
> Everything after that is second pass.
> 
> ## The trap to avoid
> 
> Bad Codex prompt:
> 
> ```text
> Help me make this whole platform production ready.
> ```
> 
> That prompt is trash. It invites scope explosion and fake progress.
> 
> Good Codex prompt:
> 
> ```text
> Make the add-user flow work end-to-end with the existing architecture.
> Do not redesign anything outside that path.
> Return:
> - root cause
> - files changed
> - commands run
> - test result
> - remaining blocker
> ```
> 
> That gets results.
> 
> ## My recommendation
> 
> Open Codex on the repo and start with exactly this sequence:
> 
> 1. **repo audit**
> 2. **boot path fix**
> 3. **add-user flow fix**
> 4. **ingest spine fix**
> 5. **smoke tests**
> 
> That’s the shortest route from “clever repo” to “actually works.”
> 
> Send me the current repo root tree and the exact startup commands you’re using, and I’ll turn this into a tight **Codex ticket pack** in the order that gives you the fastest usable win.

### User
> naything in the old constitition of qione worth keeping.

### Assistant
> I’m checking the old constitution against the current blueprint so I can tell you what’s signal versus what’s just legacy noise.

### Assistant
> Yes — but **only parts of it**.  
> Most of the old QiOne constitution feels like an earlier knowledge-system brain trying to govern a platform/runtime architecture. That mismatch is why it now feels half-useful, half-chaotic.
> 
> ## Keep
> 
> ### 1. The metadata instinct
> The old constitution had the right instinct that objects need structured metadata, not vibes. The `front_matter_schema` with things like `title`, `slug`, `created`, `updated`, `status`, `tags`, `aliases`, `sensitivity`, and `classification` is still useful as a **content-object/profile schema** layer. fileciteturn4file0
> 
> But it should be **subordinate** to the current blueprint’s canonical metadata contract, not compete with it. The blueprint is much cleaner here: canonical ID, domain prefix, short code, source path, ingest timestamp, MIME/extension, and status are the minimum gate before automation. It also separates identity, source, structural, semantic, and lifecycle metadata in a way the old file was groping toward but hadn’t fully disciplined yet. fileciteturn5file3
> 
> ### 2. Sensitivity / classification thinking
> The old sensitivity registry and visibility matrix are worth keeping as a **policy layer**. The idea that not all content should route or render the same way is good. That can still power masking, safe views, client/private handling, and legal/personal restrictions. fileciteturn4file1
> 
> Just don’t let that become the constitutional backbone. It belongs under governance/policy or app-level access behavior, not as the master ontology.
> 
> ### 3. Ignore patterns
> The ignore block is practical and reusable. That’s boring, but solid. It belongs in tooling/config, not in the constitutional core. fileciteturn3file0
> 
> ### 4. The idea behind `qi_decimal`
> There is still signal here, but not in its old form. The old constitution used `qi_decimal` as a sorting/categorical identity aid. fileciteturn4file0turn4file6  
> The current blueprint basically formalized and hardened that instinct into the **routing-first namespace standard**: namespace code tells where something belongs; QID / QiDecimal tells what exact object it is. fileciteturn5file3
> 
> So the answer is not “keep old `qi_decimal` unchanged.”  
> It’s: **keep the concept, replace the implementation with the new namespace law.**
> 
> ## Maybe keep, but demote hard
> 
> ### 5. “Realms”
> The old `realms` model — QiVault, QiPersonal, QiBusiness, QiClients, QiResearch, etc. — still has UX and organizational value. fileciteturn5file0  
> But constitutionally, it got outclassed by the new **band → domain → subdomain → object** model and one-schema-per-domain rule. fileciteturn5file3
> 
> So:
> - keep “realms” as a **view/filter/workspace metaphor**
> - do **not** keep them as the primary architectural truth
> 
> The new blueprint already gives you the grown-up version: bands, domains, subdomains, canonical schema homes, and placement rules. fileciteturn5file3
> 
> ## Drop
> 
> ### 6. The old `qid_rpc` / node-ID mentality
> The old constitution references `qid_rpc: qios_next_qid` and a Layer 5 worker claiming immutable node IDs. fileciteturn4file0  
> That is stale now. The current blueprint is explicit that **QiArchive owns registration**, canonical ID assignment, and short visible code issuance; it even says “qid has rolled into QiArchive.” fileciteturn4file4turn5file3
> 
> So don’t preserve the old QID issuance model as a parallel system. That would create the exact drift the new blueprint is trying to kill.
> 
> ### 7. Graph-poetry metadata
> Stuff like:
> - `orbit`
> - `entangled`
> - `graph_weight`
> - `summary` as auto-generated frontmatter
> - `parents/children/siblings/related` as baked-in node graph metadata  
> is too opinionated and too early to be canonical. fileciteturn4file0
> 
> Some of that may be useful in a **derived graph or knowledge app**, but it should not be constitutional law. The current blueprint is cleaner: graph is downstream and derived, never canonical. fileciteturn5file3
> 
> ### 8. The governance-layer matrix
> The L0–L7 matrix — “Root Integrity,” “Dark Matter Physics,” “Semantic Routing,” “Self-Healing,” etc. — is flavorful, but not tight enough to be architecture law. fileciteturn4file2turn4file3  
> It reads like internal metaphors for workers/orchestration, not stable doctrine.
> 
> Keep it only if you want a **developer mental model** or naming inspiration.  
> Do not keep it as constitutional structure.
> 
> ### 9. Folder law from the old constitution
> The old `directory_ast` and folder assumptions are too thin and too legacy to survive as canon. fileciteturn4file3  
> The current blueprint already replaced that with a real repo topology, runtime zones, local working directory, and explicit spine flow. fileciteturn5file3turn4file4
> 
> ## My clean verdict
> 
> ### Worth keeping
> - metadata discipline
> - sensitivity/classification policy concepts
> - ignore patterns
> - the underlying idea of decimal/routing codes
> - “realms” as UX/workspace language only
> 
> ### Worth killing
> - old QID issuance model
> - realms as primary ontology
> - graph-heavy frontmatter as canonical law
> - L0–L7 “physics” matrix as architecture truth
> - old folder AST as constitutional structure
> 
> ## Best way to salvage it
> 
> Do **not** merge the old constitution back into the new one whole. Bad move.
> 
> Instead, strip it into three buckets:
> 
> ### Bucket A — promote into current blueprint standards/policies
> - sensitivity/classification registry
> - maybe a trimmed content frontmatter profile
> 
> ### Bucket B — demote to app or content-layer config
> - realms
> - tags/aliases/context
> - workspace routing preferences
> 
> ### Bucket C — archive as legacy thinking
> - qid_rpc
> - L0–L7 governance matrix
> - graph-poetry metadata
> - old folder AST
> 
> ## Bottom line
> 
> The old constitution had **good instincts but weak boundaries**.  
> The new blueprint has **better boundaries but still needs execution**.
> 
> So yes, there’s signal worth keeping — but mostly as **supporting policy/config**, not as constitutional truth. The current blueprint is already the stronger spine. fileciteturn5file2turn4file7
> 
> If you want, I can do the next useful step: a **salvage map** with three columns — **Keep / Rewrite / Archive** — field by field from the old constitution.

### Assistant
> Yep. Use this in the IDE exactly as-is:
> 
> ```text
> You are updating the QiOS Master Blueprint by salvaging only the useful parts of an older QiOne constitution and folding them into the current blueprint cleanly.
> 
> OBJECTIVE
> 
> Create a disciplined salvage pass:
> - preserve the genuinely useful legacy material
> - integrate it into the current blueprint in the correct layer
> - do NOT reintroduce obsolete ontology, duplicate identity systems, or poetic-but-noncanonical governance junk
> 
> PRIMARY SOURCE OF TRUTH
> 
> The current blueprint is authoritative.
> The old constitution is legacy input only.
> 
> CURRENT BLUEPRINT RULES TO RESPECT
> - The blueprint repo is constitutional doctrine, not runtime code.
> - Current governing truth lives in docs/.
> - Machine-readable constraints belong in standards/ as YAML.
> - Canonical namespaces and tables belong in registry/ as YAML.
> - Do not create parallel identity systems.
> - Do not let derived layers become canonical truth.
> - Keep architecture more coherent, not more clever.
> 
> SALVAGE DECISIONS
> 
> KEEP / REWRITE / ARCHIVE as follows:
> 
> 1) KEEP and integrate:
> - the useful metadata/front-matter instinct
> - sensitivity and classification concepts
> - ignore patterns
> - the idea behind qi_decimal only insofar as it aligns with the current namespace/router model
> - realms only as optional UX/workspace/view labels, NOT as core ontology
> 
> 2) REWRITE before integrating:
> - front_matter_schema must be rewritten as a subordinate content metadata profile that sits under the current canonical metadata contract
> - sensitivity/classification must be normalized into a clean registry/policy model
> - realms must be reframed as non-canonical workspace taxonomy or UI-facing context labels, not schema/domain truth
> 
> 3) ARCHIVE / EXCLUDE entirely:
> - qid_rpc and any old QID issuance logic
> - the L0-L7 governance/matrix/layer model
> - graph-poetry metadata such as orbit, entangled, graph_weight, parents/children/siblings as canonical requirements
> - legacy folder-law / directory_ast as constitutional structure
> - anything that conflicts with one canonical identity, one schema home per domain, or QiArchive owning registration
> 
> IMPORTANT ARCHITECTURAL RULES
> 
> Do NOT:
> - redefine canonical identity
> - create a second registry or second ID issuance model
> - replace band/domain/subdomain/object with realms
> - make graph metadata canonical
> - turn front matter into the system of record
> - add vague speculative sections
> 
> Do:
> - make content metadata clearly subordinate to canonical archive/object metadata
> - keep sensitivity/classification as policy + registry, not metaphysics
> - preserve current blueprint structure and naming style
> - prefer append/patch over sprawling rewrite
> - keep everything machine-usable and audit-friendly
> 
> EXPECTED OUTPUTS
> 
> 1) Create a short salvage memo documenting:
> - what was kept
> - what was rewritten
> - what was archived
> - why
> 
> 2) Patch the blueprint by creating or updating the appropriate docs/ pages so the salvaged material is incorporated into the current constitutional structure.
> 
> 3) Create or update machine-readable YAML files in standards/ and/or registry/ for:
> - content metadata profile
> - sensitivity/classification registry
> - optional workspace/realm label registry only if it is clearly non-canonical
> 
> 4) Archive the legacy constitution as a legacy artifact reference, not active doctrine.
> 
> 5) Produce a final change summary listing:
> - files created
> - files modified
> - files intentionally not touched
> - any ambiguity that still needs a later ADR
> 
> IMPLEMENTATION GUIDANCE
> 
> A) CONTENT METADATA PROFILE
> Create a clean, reduced profile for content-oriented documents/notes/assets that may include fields like:
> - title
> - slug
> - aliases
> - tags
> - keywords
> - context
> - status
> - sensitivity
> - classification
> - summary (if clearly marked derived/noncanonical)
> - workspace_label or realm_label only if explicitly non-canonical
> 
> This profile must explicitly state:
> - canonical identity still comes from the blueprint’s current identity model
> - QiArchive / canonical ID / domain prefix / short code / source metadata remain primary
> - front matter is supportive metadata, not identity authority
> 
> B) SENSITIVITY / CLASSIFICATION
> Normalize the old sensitivity registry into a clean policy artifact.
> Preserve the useful ideas:
> - public
> - internal
> - confidential
> - restricted
> - secret
> and useful classifications like:
> - personal_health
> - personal_finance
> - legal
> - immigration
> - client_confidential
> - business_internal
> - marketing_public
> - system_darkmatter
> - other
> 
> But clean bad legacy values, malformed defaults, and inconsistent labels.
> Make sure this registry reads as current doctrine, not a raw port.
> 
> C) REALMS
> Do not preserve realms as the primary meaning universe or system ontology.
> If kept at all, define them only as optional workspace/view labels for content organization and UX filtering.
> They must not override:
> - band
> - domain
> - subdomain
> - schema placement
> - tenant isolation
> - canonical routing
> 
> D) QI_DECIMAL
> Do not revive the old qi_decimal model verbatim.
> Instead, document that the useful legacy instinct is now satisfied by the current namespace/router and naming standards.
> Only mention old qi_decimal in a migration/legacy note if needed.
> 
> E) EXCLUDED LEGACY ELEMENTS
> Explicitly note that these are archived and non-governing:
> - qid_rpc
> - node-ID worker issuance model
> - governance layer matrix L0-L7
> - graph relationship frontmatter as canonical schema
> - legacy directory AST / folder constitution
> 
> FILE PLACEMENT INTENT
> 
> Use the existing repo structure and conventions.
> Prefer this pattern unless the repo already has better-fitting files:
> - docs/... for constitutional prose
> - standards/... for machine-readable content metadata constraints
> - registry/... for sensitivity/classification registries and optional non-canonical workspace label maps
> - docs/legacy/ or similar for archived legacy constitution notes
> 
> If an ADR is required because a change affects namespace, schema authority, or topology, create one.
> If not required, do not create an ADR just for ceremony.
> 
> STYLE REQUIREMENTS
> 
> - Write in the same disciplined, constitutional, architecture-law voice as the existing blueprint
> - Be concrete, not mystical
> - No marketing language
> - No hand-wavy “future intelligence layer” fluff
> - Keep sections tight, structured, and enforceable
> 
> WORKFLOW
> 
> 1) Inspect current docs/, standards/, and registry/ structure first
> 2) Determine the best-fitting existing files to patch
> 3) Create new files only where there is no clean home
> 4) Make the patches
> 5) Return a concise summary and a keep/rewrite/archive table
> 
> FINAL DELIVERABLE FORMAT
> 
> Return:
> - summary of what you changed
> - keep/rewrite/archive table
> - list of touched files
> - any recommended follow-up ADRs or open questions
> 
> Do not redesign the whole blueprint.
> Do not drift into app implementation.
> This is a constitutional salvage and reconciliation pass only.
> ```
>

>

> ## The real takeaway
> 
> This is not “import some CSVs.”
> 
> This is:
> 
> **preserve → classify → quarantine sensitive junk → profile → dedupe → map → stage → migrate → verify**
> 
> Because mixed in with useful knowledge, you also have obvious **do-not-blindly-ingest** files like:
> 
> - `Chrome Passwords.csv`
> - `API Recovery Keys ... .csv`
> 
> Those should **not** be migrated into normal app tables. They need quarantine handling or exclusion from Supabase entirely. fileciteturn7file0

> - `qimessages_C_001.csv`  
>   fileciteturn7file0
> 
> ## What we should do with it
> 
> ### Do not import straight into final domain tables
> That would be sloppy and you’d contaminate the canonical layer with duplicates, weird types, legacy assumptions, and probably garbage rows.
> 
> ### Instead use a 4-zone migration model
> 
> #### Zone A — Raw vault
> Immutable raw CSVs, checksum them, catalog them, never overwrite.
> 
> #### Zone B — Staging schema in Supabase
> Example:
> - `staging.csv_import_batches`
> - `staging.csv_import_files`
> - `staging.raw_contacts`
> - `staging.raw_notes`
> - `staging.raw_emails`
> - `staging.raw_products`
> - `staging.raw_services`
> - `staging.raw_registry_rows`
> 
> #### Zone C — Clean / normalized views or tables
> Example:
> - `staging.norm_contacts`
> - `staging.norm_companies`
> - `staging.norm_notes`
> - `staging.norm_messages`
> - `staging.norm_services`
> 
> #### Zone D — Canonical destination schemas
> Then and only then map into:
> - `qione`
> - `qicase`
> - `qiknowledge`
> - `qivault`
> - `qitax`
> - `qihome`
> - `qiarchive`
> depending on actual ownership.
> 
> ## The hard truth
> 
> Some of these files are probably:
> 
> - duplicate exports of the same thing
> - partial exports
> - “all” exports plus filtered exports
> - already-merged derivatives
> - legacy schema experiments
> - sensitive data that should never hit app-facing tables
> 
> So this is not a single migration. It’s a **preservation and reconciliation job**.
> 
> ## The first useful deliverable
> 
> You need an **inventory + migration map** before anything else.
> 
> Here’s the IDE prompt for that:
> 
> ```text id="7ngs8v"
> Audit the legacy CSV corpus and prepare a preservation, merge, clean, and migration plan for Supabase.
> 
> Context:
> The legacy data folder contains many CSV exports, merged files, cleaned files, registries, knowledge-base extracts, CRM-like data, email corpora, and schema-support artifacts.
> This is not a direct import job.
> We need to preserve knowledge, prevent loss, quarantine sensitive files, dedupe intelligently, and map usable data into the current QiOS/QiOne architecture.
> 
> Objectives:
> 1. Inventory every CSV by probable domain and data type
> 2. Identify duplicates, partial duplicates, merged derivatives, and likely source-of-truth candidates
> 3. Flag sensitive/high-risk files that must be quarantined or excluded from normal ingest
> 4. Group files into migration families
> 5. Propose a staging schema for Supabase
> 6. Propose canonical destination schemas/tables where appropriate
> 7. Recommend preserve-only vs migrate vs archive decisions
> 8. Produce a migration order of operations
> 
> Required output:
> A. CSV inventory table with columns:
> - filename
> - probable source/system
> - probable entity type
> - sensitivity level
> - migration family
> - likely duplicate group
> - recommended action
> - notes
> 
> B. Sensitive/quarantine list
> Flag files like passwords, recovery keys, secrets, tokens, or anything that should not be imported into normal app tables.
> 
> C. Migration families
> Examples:
> - contacts / CRM
> - notes / knowledge
> - emails / messages
> - products / services / catalog
> - finance / COA / transactions
> - governance / registries / blueprint memory
> - system metadata / file tree / schema helpers
> 
> D. Supabase staging design
> Propose a staging schema with raw import tables and normalized tables/views.
> Do not import directly into final domain tables.
> 
> E. Canonical mapping proposal
> For each migration family, propose likely destination schemas/tables in the current architecture:
> - qione
> - qiarchive
> - qiknowledge
> - qicase
> - qivault
> - qitax
> - qihome
> - other only if justified
> 
> F. Preserve / migrate / archive / quarantine matrix
> Every file should get one of these dispositions.
> 
> Rules:
> - Do not assume all CSVs should be migrated
> - Do not treat merged or cleaned files as automatically authoritative
> - Prefer preserving raw originals and importing into staging first
> - Do not create parallel identity systems
> - Respect the current blueprint: one canonical home per concern, QiArchive owns file registration, derived layers are not truth sources
> - Be especially careful with credential-like exports, keys, and secret material
> 
> Deliverables:
> 1. a markdown migration report
> 2. a machine-readable CSV or JSON inventory
> 3. a recommended staging schema proposal
> 4. a prioritized action plan for the first 10 migration steps
> 
> Do not jump into implementation yet.
> This pass is for inventory, risk control, and architecture-safe migration planning.
> ```
> 
> ## My recommendation for first pass
> 
> Do these first:
> 
> 1. Quarantine secrets and credential exports.
> 2. Inventory all CSVs.
> 3. Group duplicate families.
> 4. Pick one family to prove the pipeline — probably **contacts** or **notes/knowledge**.
> 5. Build staging tables.
> 6. Import only one family end to end.
> 7. Validate before touching the rest.
> 
> That’s the move. Otherwise you’ll drown in “legacy data” and contaminate the clean architecture.
> 
> If you want, next I’ll give you the **second IDE prompt** that tells it to generate the actual Supabase **staging schema + import scripts** for the CSV migration pipeline.

> # The acceptance test for Milestone 1
> 
> Milestone 1 is done only when you can do this from your phone:
> 
> 1. open admin app
> 2. log in
> 3. see tenant list
> 4. create a user or invite one
> 5. attach that user to a tenant
> 6. assign role(s)
> 7. toggle module access
> 8. open a whitelisted table
> 9. edit a row
> 10. save it and see it persist in Supabase
> 
> If any one of those fails, Milestone 1 is **not done**.
> 
> ---
> 
> # The implementation rule
> 
> ## Do not let the frontend talk directly to everything
> The blueprint is clear that:
> - Supabase Auth is the identity layer
> - `qione.tenant_members` is the RBAC mapping
> - admin actions must authenticate with elevated roles
> - admin surfaces must not expose tenant data across boundaries
> - frontend/integration flows should not bypass the owning pipeline or domain contracts. fileciteturn9file1
> 
> So your admin app should use:
> 
> - **Supabase Auth** for session
> - **admin API / server actions / edge functions** for privileged operations
> - **whitelisted table operations**
> - **audit logging** on write
> 
> Not “just let the client use service role.” That would be stupid.
> 
> ---

>

>

> - qione.tenants
> - qione.tenant_members
> - qione.member_roles
> - qione.tenant_modules
>

> 
> Out of scope for this milestone:

> - I can attach the user to a tenant

> 
> Rules:
> - prefer boring working CRUD over elegant theory
> - prefer server-side/admin-safe mutation paths
> - keep scope tight
> - do not create parallel identity logic
> - do not bypass RBAC assumptions
> - do not spend time on fancy styling
> - return changed files, why they changed, and remaining blockers

> Good. That was the **right pass**.
> 
> But before you let it “spin up staging buckets,” there are **two places it can still screw you**:
> 
> ## What looks right
> 
> - quarantine first, not blind import
> - 4-zone model

> - preserve/archive split

> That is solid.

> ## What I do not trust yet
> 
> ### 1. Canonical destination guesses
> The line you quoted — `staging.norm_contacts -> qione.contact` — is a red flag.
> 
> In your current architecture, `qione` is the **platform kernel** for tenants, members, roles, modules, RBAC, not your generic business entity warehouse. Contacts/clients are domain-ish, closer to `qicase` or a still-to-be-locked domain model, not some random `qione.contact` table unless you have explicitly designed that.  
> So the IDE may already be **inventing tables instead of reconciling with the blueprint**.
> 
> ### 2. “Archive rules” might be too aggressive
> Some of those governance-ish CSVs are not migration targets, true.  
> But “preservation-only” is not the same as “irrelevant.” Some of them may need:
> 
> - salvage into YAML registry
> - legacy crosswalk tables
> - source provenance notes
> - mapping dictionaries for later transforms
> 
> So don’t let the IDE flatten everything into “archive and forget.”
> 
> ### 3. Contacts may not actually be the best first family
> Contacts are tempting, but they’re usually messy as hell:
> - duplicates
> - inconsistent names
> - partial company/person separation
> - phone/email normalization issues
> - no stable source key
> 
> A cleaner proof family might be:
> - **services/catalog**
> - **COA/mapping**
> - **notes/knowledge**
> depending on column quality.
> 
> Contacts are still okay, but only if the exports are actually clean enough.
>

> 
> The IDE is mixing three different things:
> 
> 1. **frontend publishable key**  
> 2. **backend service role key**  
> 3. **raw Postgres password**
> 
> Those are **not interchangeable**.
> 
> ## What it got right
> 
> Putting the **publishable key** in `apps/admin-portal/.env` is fine for the browser client.
> 
> ## What it has wrong
> 
> For the Python admin mutation path, the backend needs the **Supabase service role key**, not the raw Postgres password, because the current design is using Supabase admin/auth APIs and audited writes from `main.py`, not direct psycopg SQL. That’s also consistent with the earlier admin plan: server-side admin routes using the service key and writing audit entries to `qisys.system_events`. fileciteturn15file0
> 
> And per the blueprint, `qione` is the platform layer for tenants/RBAC/modules, Supabase Auth is the identity layer, and `qisys` owns system events. fileciteturn15file7
> 
> ## The hard rule
> 
> **Do not paste the service role key or database password into chat, the frontend env, or random AI config files.**
> 
> That includes:
> - `apps/admin-portal/.env`
> - anything `VITE_*`
> - browser-exposed config
> - “helpful” MCP cache files unless you personally intend that and understand the blast radius
> 
> ## What to do yourself, locally

>

> SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY=YOUR_ACTUAL_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY
> ```
> 
> Put it somewhere only the Python API loads.  
> Best options:
> 
> - `python/qiarchive/api/.env`
> - or a project-root backend env that the Python process explicitly loads
> 
> Do **not** use `VITE_` for this.
>

> 
> ## The actual split
> 
> You have **two different overlap clusters**.
> 
> ### Cluster A — narrative/history/content brands
> - `qistory`
> - `qsaysit`
> - `qjournal` / `qsjournal`
> 
> These are all orbiting the same thing: **Q’s lived record, voice, reflection, and publication**.
> 
> ### Cluster B — knowledge/object/intelligence system names
> - `qinode`
> - `qinote`
> - `qiobjects`
> 
> These are all trying to answer:  
> “what is the canonical thing, where is it stored, and who does the semantic work?”
> 
> That second cluster is where architecture gets sloppy fast.
> 
> ---
> 
> # My blunt consolidation call
> 
> ## 1. `QiHistory` should be the umbrella
> Kill the split between `qistory`, `qjournal`, and `qsaysit` as separate top-level conceptual worlds.
> 
> Use:
> 
> ### **QiHistory**
> The umbrella domain for **personal/historical record, narrative memory, and public-facing reflections derived from it**.
> 
> Under that:
> 
> - **QJournal** = private/raw/personal notes and reflections
> - **QsaysIt** = public/published/curated expression
> - **Qistory** = either  
>   a) rename into **QiHistory**, or  
>   b) archive as an earlier naming experiment
> 
> Do **not** keep both `qistory` and `qihistory`. That’s stupid duplication.
> 
> ## 2. `QiArchive` is **not** part of `QiHistory`
> This is important.
> 
> Do **not** fold `QiArchive` into the storytelling/history family.
> 
> The blueprint is clear that **QiArchive is the file registration and archival identity spine** and owns canonical file registration, short-code assignment, filename contract, checksum, and lineage continuity. fileciteturn17file1 fileciteturn17file2
> 
> So:
> 
> - `QiHistory` = meaning / narrative / memory domain
> - `QiArchive` = system spine for registered files and provenance
> 
> Different jobs. Keep them separate.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Second cluster: what to kill, what to keep
> 
> ## 3. `QiNote` stays
> The blueprint already defines **QiNote as the local-first note and knowledge layer**. fileciteturn17file1turn17file2
> 
> So `QiNote` should own:
> - authoring
> - note capture
> - knowledge records
> - working notes
> - drafts
> - structured note objects
> 
> That likely maps conceptually to your `qiknowledge` domain, not to some extra product sprawl. The live domain map already includes `qiknowledge`. fileciteturn17file2
> 
> ## 4. `QiNode` stays, but gets demoted to an intelligence layer
> The blueprint also already describes **QiNode as the structural and semantic intelligence layer** and explicitly leaves open “how QiNote and QiNode divide responsibility at maturity.” fileciteturn17file1turn17file2
> 
> So the answer is:
> 
> - **QiNote** = canonical note/knowledge authoring layer
> - **QiNode** = derived semantic/enrichment layer
> 
> `QiNode` should do things like:
> - entity extraction
> - semantic tagging
> - structural inference
> - retrieval assists
> - graph/relationship projections
> 
> It should **not** be another place where notes “live.”
> 
> ## 5. `QiObjects` should die as a product name
> This one is the weakest.
> 
> “Objects” is a useful internal ontology term. Your blueprint already talks about **object families**, canonical homes, placement rules, and object model concerns. fileciteturn17file1turn17file2
> 
> But `QiObjects` as a separate branded system is mostly asking for confusion.
> 
> Use **object** as an internal technical concept, not a top-level product/domain brand.
> 
> So:
> 
> - keep **object model** as architecture language
> - kill **QiObjects** as a separate product namespace unless it becomes a very specific developer tool later
> 
> ---
> 
> # The clean map
> 
> ## Keep as canonical concepts
> - **QiArchive** = registration spine
> - **QiNote** = note/knowledge authoring layer
> - **QiNode** = semantic/structural intelligence layer
> - **QiHistory** = narrative/history umbrella
> 
> ## Keep as sub-surfaces or sub-brands
> - **QJournal** = private/raw stream under QiHistory
> - **QsaysIt** = public/published voice under QiHistory / QiCMS
> 
> ## Archive / merge away
> - **Qistory** → merge into **QiHistory**
> - **QiObjects** → retire as top-level product name
> 
> ---
> 
> # The one-sentence rule for each
> 
> ### QiArchive
> Registers files. Preserves provenance. Owns canonical archival identity. fileciteturn17file1turn17file2
> 
> ### QiNote
> Stores and edits notes, knowledge, and authored working memory. fileciteturn17file1turn17file2
> 
> ### QiNode
> Derives structure and meaning from spine-backed records. It enriches; it does not become truth. That fits the blueprint’s repeated rule that graph/AI/derived layers are downstream, not canonical. fileciteturn17file1turn17file2
> 
> ### QiHistory
> Organizes the human story layer: journal, timeline, reflections, public expression.
> 
> ### QJournal
> Private source material.
> 
> ### QsaysIt
> Published derivative expression.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Why this is the right cut
> 
> Because your own architecture already says:
> 
> - one canonical home per concern
> - avoid parallel schemas / duplicate logic
> - graph and derived layers are not truth
> - the system must stay understandable fileciteturn17file1turn17file2
> 
> Right now these names are violating that.
> 
> ---
> 
> # My recommendation
>

>

> - `QiHistory` = umbrella
> - `QJournal` and `QsaysIt` = sub-surfaces
> - `QiArchive` = separate core spine, not part of history
> - `QiNote` = canonical note/knowledge layer
> - `QiNode` = derived intelligence layer
> - `QiObjects` = retire as product namespace
> 
> That is the least confusing version.

> - QiNote = authored notes, local-first note capture, knowledge records, drafts, working notes

> ## The right way to “see it”

> 
> ### 1. System stack map

> - Application: QiOne portal, tools, interfaces

> - **Cloud**

> ### 3. Data lifecycle map

> ### 5. Ownership / no-BS boundary map

> - `qione` owns tenants, members, RBAC, modules
> - `qiknowledge` owns notes

> 
> Not Figma first.  
> Not some giant hand-made masterpiece.
> 
> Start with:
> 
> - `docs/appendices/flow_atlas.md`
> 
> Inside it, create sections:
> 
> 1. System Stack
> 2. Runtime / Tech Stack
> 3. Canonical Ingestion Flow
> 4. Query / Retrieval Flow
> 5. Admin App Workflow
> 6. Service Ownership Matrix
> 7. Open Gaps / Unverified Paths
> 
> That gives you one canonical place to look.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## What I would have the IDE generate
> 
> Use this prompt:
> 
> ```text
> Create a new blueprint appendix called `docs/appendices/flow_atlas.md`.
> 
> Objective:
> Show how data, workflows, code, apps, pipelines, and tech stacks flow through the current QiOS / QiOne architecture.
> 
> Use the current blueprint as source of truth.
> Do not invent new systems.
> Do not redesign the architecture.
> Do not use vague prose where a diagram is better.
> 
> Build the appendix as a “Flow Atlas” with Mermaid diagrams and concise explanatory notes.
> 
> Required sections:
> 
> 1. System Stack Map
> Show the layered architecture:
> - Delivery
> - Application
> - Platform
> - Domain
> - Core
> 
> Use current blueprint roles:
> - Delivery: portals, web, admin, chatbot surfaces
> - Application: QiOne portal, tools, interfaces
> - Platform: qione + Supabase Auth
> - Domain: qicase, qichronicle, qicms, qihome, qiknowledge, qitax, qivault
> - Core: qiarchive, qigraph, qially, qisys
> 
> 2. Runtime / Tech Stack Map
> Show local vs cloud runtime split:
> Local:
> - file watcher
> - ingest pipeline
> - OCR
> - extraction
> - chunking
> - embeddings
> - local API
> - C:/QiData working dirs
> 
> Cloud:
> - Supabase/Postgres
> - pgvector
> - Redis
> - Neo4j
> - app APIs
> - admin surface
> 
> 3. Canonical Ingestion Flow
> Show:
> source file
> -> detect
> -> resolve domain
> -> register in qiarchive
> -> assign canonical ID / short code
> -> normalize filename
> -> extract
> -> enrich metadata
> -> chunk
> -> embed
> -> index
> -> route / review / act
> 
> 4. Query / Retrieval Flow
> Show:
> user query
> -> metadata filter in Postgres
> -> semantic retrieval in pgvector
> -> optional graph expansion
> -> assemble evidence with provenance
> -> answer / result
> 
> 5. Admin App Workflow
> Use the currently patched admin path:
> - browser/phone
> - admin portal frontend
> - Supabase Auth session / JWT
> - Python FastAPI admin route
> - approved mutation path
> - qione table writes
> - qisys.system_events audit log
> 
> Mark clearly what is:
> - implemented
> - partially implemented
> - still unverified
> 
> 6. Service Ownership Matrix
> Show who owns what and what each service explicitly does NOT own.
> 
> 7. Open Gaps / Unverified Paths
> Include honest notes for:
> - any unverified admin mutation path
> - any incomplete invite flow
> - any open ingestion/review gaps
> - any places where the blueprint is defined but code is not yet real
> 
> Rules:
> - Keep it concrete
> - Use Mermaid for every major flow
> - Keep explanatory text tight
> - Use blueprint vocabulary
> - Do not invent endpoints or schema tables not already supported
> - Mark unverified paths as unverified
> 
> After generating the file, update mkdocs navigation only if appendices are already being surfaced there.
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> ## The smart sequencing
> 
> Do it in this order:
> 
> 1. **System Stack Map**
> 2. **Runtime / Tech Stack**
> 3. **Ingestion Flow**
> 4. **Admin App Workflow**
> 5. **Ownership Matrix**
> 6. **Query / Retrieval Flow**
> 
> Because right now your biggest need is not “pretty architecture art.”
> 
> It is:
> - what runs where
> - what talks to what
> - what owns what
> - what is real vs unverified
> 
> That’s how you make the blueprint operational instead of decorative.
> 
> If you want, next I’ll give you the **Mermaid skeleton** for all 5 diagrams so the IDE has less room to freestyle.

### User
> real quick what is the only files and folders required and allowed at qilabs root.

### Assistant
> I’m pulling the exact root-topology rule from the blueprint so I can give you the hard allowed list, not a guess.

### Assistant
> Per the blueprint, the **current intended root folders** are:
> 
> - `apps/`
> - `sites/`
> - `python_local/`
> - `python_core/`
> - `scripts/`
> - `workers/`
> - `packages/`
> - `docs/`
> - `supabase/` **only if still needed as a transitional/integrational area** fileciteturn20file1 fileciteturn20file10
> 
> And the hard rule is: **no undocumented root directories without an ADR**. fileciteturn20file2
> 
> ## But there is blueprint drift
> Another part of the same blueprint still says the canonical repo topology is:
> 
> - `/apps`
> - `/sites`
> - `/python/qiarchive`
> - `/workers`
> - `/packages`
> - `/infra`
> - `/data` fileciteturn20file2
> 
> So right now your doctrine is **internally inconsistent** on root topology.
> 
> ## My clean call
> If you want the **practical current lock**, use this as the only allowed QiLabs root folders:
> 
> ```text
> apps/
> sites/
> python_local/
> python_core/
> scripts/
> workers/
> packages/
> docs/
> supabase/   # only while transitional; remove later if not needed
> ```
> 
> Everything else at root should be treated as:
> - temporary
> - legacy
> - or requiring an ADR before it stays
> 
> ## For root files
> The blueprint does **not** give a full explicit allowlist for root files. So don’t let anyone pretend it does.
> 
> The only sane root files are the usual control-plane files, like:
> - `README.md`
> - `mkdocs.yml`
> - `.gitignore`
> - workspace/package manifests
> - CI/config files
> 
> But the **folder** list above is the part the blueprint actually states. fileciteturn20file1turn20file10
> 
> ## Blunt truth
> You need to patch the blueprint and pick **one** root model. Right now it’s split between:
> - `python_local/python_core`
> and
> - `python/qiarchive + infra + data`
> 
> That drift needs to die.

### Assistant
> You do **not** want a “clean everything” script. That’s how you nuke live work.  
> You want a **move-only consolidator** with **dry-run first**, **manifest logging**, and a **timestamped `.trash` quarantine**.

### Assistant
> Yes. And the safe answer is:
> 
> ## Do **not** use a delete script  
> Use a **move-only reorg script** that does 4 things:
> 
> 1. defaults to **dry-run**
> 2. moves everything into `C:\QiLabs\.trash\<timestamp>\...`
> 3. writes a **manifest log**
> 4. never overwrites target files during merges
> 
> I made you a starter script and a sample plan:
> 
> - [safe_reorg.py](sandbox:/mnt/data/safe_reorg.py)
> - [reorg_plan.sample.json](sandbox:/mnt/data/reorg_plan.sample.json)
> 
> ## What this script does
> 
> It supports two useful modes:
> 
> ### 1. `scan-trash`
> For obvious junk like:
> - `__pycache__`
> - repo-local `venv`
> - `*.merge_*`
> - `* (2).*`
> - `* (3).*`
> 
> ### 2. `apply --plan`
> For **explicit** folder merges and trash moves you approve.
> 
> ### Safety behavior
> - **dry-run by default**
> - all moved files go to `.trash/<timestamp>/...`
> - every action is logged to:
>   - `.trash/<timestamp>/_logs/manifest.jsonl`
>   - `.trash/<timestamp>/_logs/summary.csv`
> - on merge conflict, it **does not overwrite**
> - identical duplicates get quarantined
> - restore is supported from the manifest
> 
> ---
> 
> # The move
> 
> ## First pass: only clean obvious garbage
> From your tree, this is the highest-confidence cleanup:
> 
> - `__pycache__/`
> - repo `venv/`
> - `README.md.merge_*`
> - `package-lock (2).json`
> - `tailwind.config (2).js`
> - `tsconfig (2).json`
> - `vite.config (2).ts`
> - `index (2).html`
> - `index (3).html`
> - `App (2).css`
> - obvious numbered duplicate artifacts
> 
> That gets you a lot of visual relief **without touching real work**.
> 
> ### Run this first
> ```powershell
> python C:\path\to\safe_reorg.py scan-trash `
>   --root C:\QiLabs `
>   --patterns `
>     "**/__pycache__" `
>     "**/venv" `
>     "**/*.merge_*" `
>     "**/* (2).*" `
>     "**/* (3).*" `
>     "**/*.bak" `
>     "**/*.tmp" `
>     "**/*.backup" `
>   --ignore `
>     "**/.git/**" `
>     "**/node_modules/**" `
>     "**/.trash/**"
> ```
> 
> That is a **dry-run**.
> 
> Then inspect the log it prints.
> 
> If it looks right, run the same thing with `--execute`.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Second pass: explicit merges only
> This is where you use a plan file.
> 
> Example:
> ```powershell
> python C:\path\to\safe_reorg.py apply `
>   --root C:\QiLabs `
>   --plan C:\path\to\reorg_plan.sample.json
> ```
> 
> Again, dry-run first.  
> Then:
> 
> ```powershell
> python C:\path\to\safe_reorg.py apply `
>   --root C:\QiLabs `
>   --plan C:\path\to\reorg_plan.sample.json `
>   --execute
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> # Restore if something was moved wrong
> ```powershell
> python C:\path\to\safe_reorg.py restore `
>   --manifest C:\QiLabs\.trash\20260401_123456\_logs\manifest.jsonl
> ```
> 
> Dry-run first. Add `--execute` when ready.
> 
> ---
> 
> # What I would **not** merge automatically from your tree
> 
> These are **human decision** merges, not script-first merges:
> 
> - `qinode` vs `qinote`
> - `qistory` / `qjournal` / `qsaysit`
> - `config/` vs `bootstrap/config/`
> - `docs/__QiOS_Master_Blueprint_v0.4` vs `__QiOS_Master_Blueprint`
> - `admin-portal` vs `qione-portal`
> - old phase folders like `app_QiNoteOS_ph*`
> 
> Why: those are not mechanical duplicates. They are **competing concepts and partially progressed codebases**.
> 
> Use the script there only after you decide:
> - canonical keeper
> - quarantine target
> - merge review target
> 
> ---
> 
> # My recommended cleanup order
> 
> ## Wave 1 — safe junk purge
> Use `scan-trash` only for:
> - caches
> - numbered duplicates
> - merge artifacts
> - local venvs inside repo
> 
> ## Wave 2 — legacy quarantine
> Move whole obviously dead legacy folders into `.trash_review/` or `.trash/<stamp>/legacy/` **one at a time**, only after you name the keeper.
> 
> ## Wave 3 — explicit content/code merges
> Use `merge_dir` actions only for:
> - docs
> - templates
> - content folders
> - assets you’ve already chosen to consolidate
> 
> Not for active app code unless you’ve already picked the canonical app.
> 
> ---
> 
> # The blunt truth from your tree
> 
> You do **not** have one problem. You have three:
> 
> ## 1. junk artifacts
> Easy to quarantine with the script
> 
> ## 2. legacy project sprawl
> Needs bucket decisions
> 
> ## 3. ontology collisions
> Needs human naming decisions before code merges
> 
> So the script should solve **#1 immediately** and help with **#2 carefully**.  
> It should **not** pretend to solve **#3** by brute force.
> 
> ---
> 
> # The safest rule for this repo
> 
> Anything the script touches should be one of these:
> 
> - clearly rebuildable
> - clearly duplicated
> - clearly generated
> - clearly legacy and already superseded
> - explicitly listed in a plan you approved
> 
> If it doesn’t fit one of those, don’t automate it.
> 
> ---
> 
> # My recommendation for your exact next move
> 
> Run the junk-only dry-run first.
> 
> Then send me the `summary.csv` categories or a screenshot of the dry-run output, and I’ll help you turn that into a **Wave 1 approved execute set** without letting the script eat live work.

> ## The distinction
> 
> What I was recommending is **flattening clutter**, not flattening architecture.
> 
> ### Not allowed
> Do **not** flatten:
> - `apps/`
> - `packages/`
> - `python/`
> - `services/`
> - `workers/`
> - `sites/`
> - `docs/`
> - domain/app boundaries
> - repo topology
> 
> That would destroy separation of concerns and make the repo worse.
> 
> ### Allowed
> What I *was* recommending is safely quarantining or consolidating things like:
> - `__pycache__`
> - repo-local `venv/`
> - `*.merge_*`
> - `* (2).*`
> - `* (3).*`
> - obvious duplicate generated files
> - dead legacy copies after you pick a keeper
> 
> That is **cleanup**, not repo flattening.
> 
> ## Where the confusion came from
> 
> You pasted a tree that includes a bunch of old scripts with names like:
> - `folder_flatten.py`
> - `dir_flatten.py`
> - `inbox_flatten.ps1`
> 
> Those exist in your repo, but I was **not** telling you to run a global flatten pass on `_QiOne_MonoRepo`.
> 
> And if my wording sounded like “flatten the repo,” that was sloppy. The correct rule is:
> 
> > **Preserve the monorepo structure. Flatten only intake clutter or duplicate junk inside controlled zones.**
> 
> ## The right policy
> 
> ### `_QiOne_MonoRepo`
> Keep structured.
> 
> ### `QiData/inbox`, dropzones, scans, downloads-like holding areas
> These can be normalized or lightly flattened **inside those intake zones** if needed.
> 
> ### legacy project graveyard / duplicate experiments
> These can be moved to `.trash/<timestamp>/...` or `legacy_quarantine/` after a keeper is chosen.
> 
> ## So the script should be used for this
> 
> ### Safe now
> - move obvious junk to `.trash`
> - move duplicate-name artifacts to `.trash`
> - quarantine dead branches/copies you already know are not the keeper
> 
> ### Not safe now
> - collapsing app folders together
> - merging unrelated products automatically
> - flattening all nested folders under `_QiOne_MonoRepo`
> - “one big repo cleanup” against active code
> 
> ## The corrected rule set
> 
> Use the script in **3 modes only**:
> 
> ### 1. Trash mode
> For obvious garbage only.
> 
> ### 2. Quarantine mode
> Move old candidates out of the way without deleting them.
> 
> ### 3. Merge mode
> Only after you explicitly decide:
> - keeper
> - source to retire
> - destination
> - conflict policy
> 
> ## What I recommend now
> 
> For `_QiOne_MonoRepo`, do **not** run anything called flatten.
> 
> Do this instead:
> 
> 1. **Trash obvious junk**
> 2. **Quarantine duplicate apps/legacy copies**
> 3. **Decide canonical keepers**
> 4. **Merge only selected folders into those keepers**
> 5. Leave the monorepo topology intact
> 
> ## The real problem in your tree
> 
> Your repo is not suffering from “too much nesting.”
> 
> It is suffering from:
> - duplicate products
> - legacy copies
> - parallel experiments
> - generated junk
> - unclear canonical keepers
> 
> That is a **selection and quarantine problem**, not a flatten-everything problem.
> 
> If you want, I’ll give you a **safer revised plan file** that only does:
> - junk-to-trash
> - duplicate-artifact quarantine
> - no structural flattening at all.
