### Assistant
> I’m pulling the blueprint itself so I can patch this against the actual source instead of guessing. After that I’ll give you the exact blueprint changes, Supabase additions, and where compliance gets enforced.

### Assistant
> I’ve got the blueprint rules now. Next I’m reconciling them against the repo artifacts you uploaded so I can tell you the actual patch, not the aspirational one.

### Assistant
> Yep. Here’s the clean answer.
> 
> ## The important correction
> 
> **2990 is not your customer ceiling.**  
> It only becomes a ceiling if you make the mistake of using **band slots as customer IDs**. Do not do that.
> 
> In the blueprint, bands and namespace routes are **routing structure**, not row cardinality. The blueprint already says the namespace model is routing-first, the system registry governs issuance, every domain table must carry `tenant_id`, and automation halts until required metadata exists. fileciteturn26file1 fileciteturn25file11 fileciteturn25file7
> 
> So the fix is:
> 
> - **Band / QiDecimal** = structural placement
> - **tenant_id / client_id / canonical_id** = real entity identity
> - **domain_prefix** = namespace owner marker
> - **auth.users / tenant_members** = access, not namespace
> 
> That is the core patch.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## What the blueprint needs to say explicitly
> 
> You need to lock one rule that is currently implied but not explicit enough:
> 
> ### New doctrine rule
> **Namespace allocations are not the same thing as tenants, clients, contacts, or users.**
> 
> A prefix is issued only to an entity that **owns a namespace**.
> 
> That means:
> 
> - a **tenant** may get a primary prefix
> - a **client** may get a prefix **only if** they are treated as a namespace-owning root
> - a **contact** does **not** get a prefix
> - an **auth user/login** does **not** get a prefix
> - a **document/file** does **not** get a new prefix; it inherits one
> - a **case** usually inherits tenant/client namespace unless you deliberately promote matters to namespace roots
> 
> That resolves the “13,000 contacts / future logins” problem immediately.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Full blueprint patch
> 
> You do **not** need to rewrite the whole blueprint. You need a targeted patch across these sections.
> 
> ### 1. `docs/01_governance/registry.md`
> Add a new subsection:
> 
> **Registry Authority Split**
> - YAML in `registry/` remains the human-reviewed constitutional map
> - Supabase `registry` schema is the operational mirror used by runtime, APIs, and pipelines
> - YAML and DB must reconcile; neither is allowed to drift silently
> - band registry and prefix registry are related but not interchangeable
> 
> Also add:
> 
> **Namespace Owner Rule**
> - only namespace-owning entities receive prefixes
> - contacts, users, and memberships inherit namespace; they do not allocate one
> 
> The blueprint already establishes registry authority and prefix mapping, but it is too thin on owner semantics. fileciteturn24file1
> 
> ### 2. `docs/01_governance/standards.md`
> Add:
> 
> **Prefix Issuance Standard**
> - `domain_prefix` is issued once per namespace root
> - prefix is immutable after issuance except explicit retirement/supersession
> - prefix format is human-recognizable and machine-validatable
> - prefix collisions are prevented by registry allocation, not naming convention luck
> 
> **Non-Issuance Rule**
> - do not issue prefixes for auth users, contacts, cases, notes, files, jobs, modules, or embeddings
> 
> ### 3. `docs/02_architecture/identity.md`
> Add a hard separation model:
> 
> - `auth.users` = identity/login
> - `qione.tenant_members` = access mapping
> - `qione.tenants` = workspace/container
> - `qicase.clients` = business/client entity
> - `registry.namespace_allocations` = namespace ownership
> - optional 1:1 between tenant and primary prefix
> - optional 1:1 between client and prefix **only when client is namespace-owning**
> 
> The blueprint already says auth is delegated to Supabase and tenant tables are the platform container. Make that relationship explicit. fileciteturn24file1
> 
> ### 4. `docs/03_structure/bands.md`
> Add:
> 
> **Band Cardinality Rule**
> - band nodes are structural buckets, not entity counters
> - `3000CLT` or similar is a route family, not “max number of clients”
> - customer scale is determined by DB rows and prefix allocations, not QiDecimal slot count
> 
> This is the exact place to kill the “2990 clients max” confusion.
> 
> ### 5. `docs/03_structure/placement_rules.md`
> Add:
> 
> **When a client gets its own prefix**
> - yes: standalone client workspace, external tenant root, client-owned document realm, isolated portal tenancy
> - no: simple CRM contact, dependent person, one of many people under same client/org, login-only user
> 
> ### 6. `docs/04_data/schema.md`
> Lock the runtime registry model and cross-links.
> 
> ### 7. `docs/04_data/metadata.md`
> Clarify:
> - `domain_prefix` is required on routed canonical records and inherited objects
> - inherited namespace is allowed
> - generated/derived layers cannot invent new prefixes
> 
> The automation gate is already there; this patch makes inheritance explicit. fileciteturn25file7
> 
> ### 8. `docs/05_compute/pipelines.md`
> Make prefix resolution a hard gate:
> - detect
> - checksum
> - resolve namespace owner
> - resolve prefix
> - register archive record
> - only then proceed
> 
> The blueprint already says resolve domain/namespace before registration. Keep that and wire it harder. fileciteturn26file6 fileciteturn26file3
> 
> ### 9. `docs/06_applications/tools.md`
> Define the compliance tooling contract:
> - repo validator
> - DB validator
> - pipeline gate
> - admin mutation guard
> 
> ### 10. New ADR
> Create something like:
> 
> **`ADR-0006-prefix-allocation-and-namespace-owner-model.md`**
> 
> That ADR should lock:
> - namespace owner concept
> - tenant/contact/login separation
> - prefix issuance rules
> - inheritance rules
> - why band slots are not customer count
> 
> Per the blueprint, schema/namespace/topology changes should be ADR-backed. fileciteturn24file0
> 
> ---
> 
> ## What needs to be added to Supabase
> 
> You already appear to have the beginnings of this in the repo:
> 
> - `0007_namespace_registry.sql`
> - `0014_registry.namespace_allocations.sql`
> - `0015_registry_namespace_bands.sql` fileciteturn26file2 fileciteturn26file4
> 
> So do **not** invent a second registry. Finish the one that’s already there.
> 
> ## The runtime model you want
> 
> ### A. `registry.namespace_bands`
> Purpose: map schema/domain to structural band.
> 
> Suggested minimum:
> - `band_code` text pk
> - `band_label` text
> - `sort_order` int
> - `is_active` bool
> 
> If this table already exists, keep it.
> 
> ### B. `registry.namespace_allocations`
> This should be the real prefix ledger.
> 
> Suggested minimum columns:
> - `allocation_id uuid pk`
> - `prefix_code text unique not null`
> - `owner_kind text not null`
>   - allowed: `tenant`, `client`, `realm`, `internal`, `system`
> - `owner_id uuid not null`
> - `display_name text not null`
> - `tenant_id uuid null`
> - `schema_name text null`
> - `subdomain text null`
> - `is_primary boolean default true`
> - `status text not null default 'active'`
>   - `active`, `retired`, `reserved`, `superseded`
> - `issued_at timestamptz not null default now()`
> - `issued_by uuid null`
> - `retired_at timestamptz null`
> - `notes text null`
> 
> Critical constraints:
> - unique(`prefix_code`)
> - unique(`owner_kind`,`owner_id`) where `is_primary = true`
> - check `owner_kind` enum
> - FK to `qione.tenants` when `tenant_id` present
> 
> ### C. `registry.subdomain_registry`  
> Optional but useful.
> - `schema_name`
> - `subdomain_key`
> - `description`
> - `is_active`
> 
> ### D. Extend `qione.tenants`
> Add:
> - `primary_namespace_allocation_id uuid null`
> - or `primary_domain_prefix text unique null`
> 
> I prefer FK to allocation table, not duplicated text.
> 
> ### E. Extend `qicase.clients`
> Add:
> - `namespace_allocation_id uuid null`
> 
> Nullable on purpose.  
> Because **not every client needs a root prefix**.
> 
> ### F. Ensure canonical/archive records carry inherited namespace
> On `qiarchive.archive_files` and other routed canonical tables, keep:
> - `tenant_id`
> - `domain_prefix`
> - `subdomain`
> - `route_state`
> - `review_state`
> 
> The blueprint already expects `domain_prefix` in metadata and `tenant_id` in domain-scoped records. fileciteturn25file7 fileciteturn25file9
> 
> ---
> 
> ## What function checks compliance
> 
> Not one function. **Three gates.**
> 
> ### 1. Repo/build compliance
> **`scripts/enforce_structure.py`**
> 
> This is your doctrine/topology validator. The blueprint already names it as the machine validation tool. fileciteturn25file11 fileciteturn24file0
> 
> It should validate:
> - schema ↔ band registry consistency
> - YAML registry consistency
> - forbidden root dirs
> - namespace file naming rules
> - no undocumented new schema/domain
> 
> ### 2. Runtime namespace compliance
> Create a DB function like:
> 
> **`registry.validate_namespace_assignment(...)`**
> 
> Suggested signature:
> ```sql
> registry.validate_namespace_assignment(
>   p_owner_kind text,
>   p_owner_id uuid,
>   p_tenant_id uuid,
>   p_schema_name text,
>   p_domain_prefix text,
>   p_subdomain text default null
> ) returns jsonb
> ```
> 
> It should check:
> - prefix exists
> - prefix is active
> - prefix belongs to the claimed owner
> - schema belongs to the declared band
> - domain-scoped rows have tenant_id
> - subdomain is valid if supplied
> - owner/prefix/tenant combination is legal
> 
> Return shape:
> ```json
> {
>   "ok": true,
>   "errors": [],
>   "resolved_band": "b_domain",
>   "resolved_namespace_path": "b_domain.qicase.civil.case_record"
> }
> ```
> 
> ### 3. Pipeline automation gate
> Create:
> 
> **`qiarchive.is_automation_ready(p_archive_id uuid)`**
> 
> This is the implementation of the blueprint’s metadata gate.  
> It checks:
> - canonical_id
> - domain_prefix
> - short_code
> - source_path
> - ingest_timestamp
> - mime_type
> - status
> 
> If any are missing, pipeline stops. That is already blueprint law. fileciteturn25file7
> 
> ---
> 
> ## When do we run these checks
> 
> ### Run namespace validation:
> - when creating a tenant
> - when issuing a new prefix
> - when promoting a client to namespace-owning root
> - when registering a file into `qiarchive`
> - when confirming a route/review
> - when admin tools mutate namespace-sensitive records
> 
> ### Run automation gate:
> - before extraction
> - before chunking
> - before embedding
> - before graph projection
> - before AI retrieval exposure
> 
> ### Do **not** issue a new prefix:
> - for a new contact
> - for a new auth login
> - for a tenant member row
> - for every case
> - for every uploaded document
> - for every portal user
> 
> ### Do **not** bypass validation:
> - from frontend writes
> - from ad hoc scripts
> - from admin table browser
> - from imports/connectors
> 
> The blueprint is already explicit that frontends/integrations cannot bypass pipeline ownership and that admin mutations need audited elevated paths. fileciteturn24file1 fileciteturn26file13
> 
> ---
> 
> ## The tenant/contact/login model you should lock
> 
> This is the part your IDE needs to understand.
> 
> ### Correct model
> 
> **Tenant**
> - the workspace/container
> - one tenant can have many contacts
> - one tenant can have many logins
> - one tenant usually has one primary prefix
> 
> **Client**
> - business/person record in ops domain
> - may or may not own its own namespace
> - can be linked to a tenant
> 
> **Contact**
> - person associated with a client
> - no prefix
> - no tenant of their own unless promoted
> 
> **Auth user**
> - login identity
> - no prefix
> - linked through `tenant_members`
> 
> **tenant_member**
> - access mapping only
> - no prefix
> - can belong to multiple tenants
> 
> That means:
> 
> - **13,000 contacts** does **not** mean 13,000 tenants
> - **13,000 future logins** does **not** mean 13,000 prefixes
> - only namespace-owning containers consume prefix allocations
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Example so it clicks
> 
> ### Example 1: one business client, many people
> - Tenant: **Built By Rays**
> - Prefix: `BBR4821`
> - Contacts: Bob, Jane, Carlos, Sarah
> - Logins: Bob + Jane
> - Cases: 4
> - Documents: 800
> 
> Result:
> - 1 tenant
> - 1 prefix
> - 4 contacts
> - 2 auth users
> - 2 tenant_members
> - 800 files inheriting `BBR4821`
> 
> ### Example 2: one person becomes their own workspace
> - Tenant: **Zai**
> - Prefix: `ZJK4566`
> 
> Result:
> - new tenant
> - new prefix
> - still only one prefix for that namespace root
> 
> ### Example 3: contact without namespace
> - Client: “Maria Lopez”
> - Contact row exists
> - invited to portal under existing tenant
> 
> Result:
> - no new prefix
> - no new tenant
> - just `auth.users` + `tenant_members`
> 
> ---
> 
> ## What to tell the IDE to update
> 
> Tell it this, bluntly:
> 
> 1. **Stop treating CLT/band slots as entity count.**
> 2. **Treat `tenant_id` as the real workspace container.**
> 3. **Treat `domain_prefix` as a namespace allocation, not a user/contact ID.**
> 4. **Only namespace-owning entities get prefixes.**
> 5. **Contacts and auth users inherit namespace.**
> 6. **All runtime namespace checks go through `registry.validate_namespace_assignment()`.**
> 7. **All pipeline advancement goes through `qiarchive.is_automation_ready()`.**
> 8. **Repo/build compliance stays in `scripts/enforce_structure.py`.**
> 9. **Use the existing registry migrations as the base, not a second registry system.** fileciteturn26file2 fileciteturn26file4
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Bottom line
> 
> Your architecture is fine. The confusion is in the **mapping**, not the concept.
> 
> The real patch is:
> 
> - **bands = routing**
> - **prefixes = namespace ownership**
> - **tenants = containers**
> - **contacts/users = memberships/identities**
> - **DB row count is not limited by QiDecimal structure**
> 
> That is the fix.
> 
> If you want, I’ll turn this into the exact **ADR text + Supabase migration spec + IDE instruction patch** in one clean block.

### User
> why is there all the sites in apps, then a root level sites folder.

### Assistant
> I’m checking the tree against the blueprint language so I can tell you whether this is intentional layering or just repo drift.

### Assistant
> I found the mismatch. This is mostly not a “design choice,” it’s a transition/drift problem with one real structural distinction underneath it.

### Assistant
> Because the repo is **mixed between the intended structure and transitional drift**.
> 
> ## What it *should* mean
> 
> The blueprint explicitly says the monorepo should have **both** `/apps` and `/sites` at the root. It also distinguishes app surfaces from web/delivery surfaces: `/apps` is for user-facing application surfaces like portal, admin, search, review UI, chatbot UI, and controls, while `/sites` is part of the canonical top-level topology for site-style builds. fileciteturn27file7 fileciteturn28file1
> 
> So the real distinction is:
> 
> - **`/apps`** = interactive application products  
>   Examples: admin portal, tenant portal, APIs, authenticated tools
> - **`/sites`** = public or mostly static web properties  
>   Examples: marketing sites, docs sites, landing pages, content sites
> 
> ## What your tree is actually showing
> 
> Your tree has a bunch of clearly site-like projects living under `apps`:
> - `docs-site`
> - `qially-flows`
> - `qially-me`
> - `qially-web` fileciteturn27file1 fileciteturn27file9
> 
> And then it also has a **root-level `sites/` folder with `qially-me` in it**, which means at least one of these is duplicated or mid-move. fileciteturn27file0 fileciteturn27file2
> 
> That is not a clean intentional pattern. That is **repo drift**.
> 
> ## Blunt answer
> 
> There are sites in `apps` **because the repo is not fully normalized yet**.
> 
> You’ve got:
> 1. the **correct architectural idea**: separate apps from sites,
> 2. but the **current implementation is inconsistent**,
> 3. and at least one project (`qially-me`) appears to exist in both places, which is exactly the kind of “one concern, two homes” drift the blueprint says not to allow. fileciteturn28file1 fileciteturn28file2
> 
> ## What belongs where
> 
> ### Keep in `apps`
> - `admin-portal`
> - `qione`
> - `qione-portal`
> - `api`
> - probably `dashy` if you’re using it as an operational app/tool rather than a published public site
> 
> ### Move to `sites`
> - `qially-web`
> - `qially-me`
> - `qially-flows`
> - probably `docs-site` if it is a published website, not internal doctrine
> 
> ### Special note on `docs-site`
> This one is awkward:
> - if it is the **actual published documentation website**, it can live in `sites/`
> - if it is the **canonical blueprint content**, that belongs under root `docs/` and not as a separate pseudo-app/site home
> 
> ## The clean interpretation
> 
> So the answer is:
> 
> **`/apps` and `/sites` both exist on purpose.  
> Site projects showing up inside `/apps` does not. That part is drift.**
> 
> The clean rule should be:
> 
> - **app has auth, state, business workflows, operator/client interaction** → `apps/`
> - **site is public/static/content/marketing/docs delivery** → `sites/`
> 
> And no project should live in both.
> 
> If you want, I’ll give you the exact **move map** for every current project: `keep`, `move to /sites`, `move to /docs`, or `archive`.

### User
> having the mono repo nested inside qilabs then the rest in mono repo seem slike a added level for our own confort not realy, i ay we move all the mono repo to the root qilab is really the mono repo.

### Assistant
> I’m checking the blueprint language against your instinct, because this is either a justified workspace layer or dead weight.

### Assistant
> The blueprint backs your instinct. It describes one canonical root, not “workspace folder → monorepo folder → actual repo.”

### Assistant
> Yes. I think your call is right.
> 
> ## Straight answer
> 
> If **QiLabs is the real working root**, then `QiLabs/_QiOne_MonoRepo/...` is mostly an extra wrapper layer, not meaningful architecture.
> 
> The blueprint describes a **single canonical repo root** that directly contains things like `apps/`, `sites/`, `python_local/`, `python_core/`, `scripts/`, `workers/`, `packages/`, and `docs/` — not “workspace folder, then actual monorepo inside it.” fileciteturn31file0 fileciteturn30file3
> 
> It also explicitly says repo topology should follow the canonical root layout and that undocumented root structures should not exist without an ADR. fileciteturn31file1
> 
> And your current working paths really are nested like `C:\QiLabs\_QiOne_MonoRepo\apps\admin-portal\src`, so this is not theoretical drift — it’s real path depth in the live repo. fileciteturn31file3
> 
> ## My recommendation
> 
> **Promote the monorepo contents to `QiLabs` root.**  
> Meaning:
> 
> - `C:\QiLabs\apps`
> - `C:\QiLabs\sites`
> - `C:\QiLabs\packages`
> - `C:\QiLabs\workers`
> - `C:\QiLabs\docs`
> - `C:\QiLabs\infra`
> - `C:\QiLabs\python_local`
> - `C:\QiLabs\python_core`
> 
> And kill the extra `_QiOne_MonoRepo` wrapper.
> 
> ## Why this is the better move
> 
> - less path depth
> - less cognitive clutter
> - less “where is the real root?” confusion
> - cleaner tooling assumptions
> - cleaner alignment with the blueprint’s root model
> - better for ADHD because the repo root becomes the actual thing, not a shell around the thing
> 
> ## The only good reason **not** to do it
> 
> Keep the nesting **only if** `QiLabs` is intentionally a higher-order workspace that will contain **multiple sibling repos** with equal status, like:
> 
> - `QiLabs/qios-master-blueprint`
> - `QiLabs/qione-platform`
> - `QiLabs/qitools`
> - `QiLabs/client-workspaces`
> 
> But that is **not** how you’re treating it right now. Right now, `QiLabs` is basically already the home of the main system. So pretending `_QiOne_MonoRepo` is the “real” root is just adding drag.
> 
> ## Clean rule
> 
> Use this:
> 
> - **QiLabs = physical repo root**
> - **QiOne = primary platform/domain inside that repo**, not the wrapper folder name
> 
> That keeps “QiLabs” as the container identity and “QiOne” as an implementation layer/product identity, which is cleaner.
> 
> ## What must be updated when you flatten it
> 
> Not the whole universe. Just the path-sensitive stuff:
> 
> - IDE workspace settings
> - scripts with hardcoded `C:\QiLabs\_QiOne_MonoRepo\...`
> - docs and README examples
> - CI/CD paths
> - Docker/build context paths
> - Supabase/local tooling paths
> - any `.env` values or watcher configs pointing at the nested root
> 
> This is a **root normalization move**, not a redesign of the internal architecture.
> 
> ## Bottom line
> 
> You’re not wrong.  
> This looks like **comfort nesting**, not real structural necessity.
> 
> **QiLabs should be the monorepo root.**  
> Flatten it and move on.
> 
> Next clean decision after that is to lock the exact canonical top-level tree under `QiLabs` and stop letting stray roots multiply.

### User
> and it looks like we are seperating the qilabs by function, like qidata, bootstrap, so it almost makes sense to do blueperint, bootstrap, tools, app, backend, workers, ect as the main folders no

### Assistant
> I’m checking this against the blueprint structure so I don’t give you a “sounds right” answer that breaks the repo later. The real question is whether the top level should reflect **domain/function** or **brand/project buckets**.

### Assistant
> There’s a clean distinction here: some of the names you’re proposing are **real architectural boundaries**, and some are just **work-phase labels** wearing folder costumes. I’m pulling the exact rule language so we can separate the two.

### Assistant
> Yes — **function/ownership should drive the top level**, not brand buckets or fuzzy concepts.
> 
> But your exact proposed names are **half right**.
> 
> ## The rule
> 
> The blueprint is explicit that repo structure should be organized **by function, not theme**, and that root directories must follow a canonical topology unless you intentionally change it with an ADR. fileciteturn33file1
> 
> It also separates the system into real layers and responsibilities — delivery, application, platform, domain, core — instead of “whatever feels related.” fileciteturn33file1
> 
> So your instinct is correct, but the root folders need to reflect **stable architectural boundaries**, not **project phases** or **narrative labels**.
> 
> ## What that means in practice
> 
> ### Good top-level folders
> These are real boundaries:
> 
> - `apps/` — user-facing app surfaces
> - `sites/` — public/static web properties
> - `workers/` — async/background jobs
> - `packages/` — shared libraries/types/utils
> - `infra/` — deployment, env, docker, ops config
> - `docs/` — blueprint, ADRs, standards, governance
> - `scripts/` — one-off or operational scripts
> - `python_local/` — machine-local runtime/control plane
> - `python_core/` — reusable Python library/toolbox
> 
> That is basically where the blueprint already points: canonical top-level structure includes `apps`, `sites`, `python_local`, `python_core`, `scripts`, `workers`, `packages`, `docs`, plus infra/data-type concerns depending on the version and transition state. fileciteturn32file0 fileciteturn33file1
> 
> ## Bad top-level folders
> These sound useful but are structurally sloppy:
> 
> - `blueprint/`
> - `bootstrap/`
> - `backend/`
> - `qidata/`
> - `tools/` as a giant catch-all root
> 
> Why they’re weak:
> 
> ### `blueprint/`
> That is **documentation/governance**, not a runtime boundary. It belongs under `docs/blueprint` or in a separate doctrine repo. The blueprint itself even says the doctrine repo is not runtime code. fileciteturn33file0
> 
> ### `bootstrap/`
> That is a **phase**, not a system boundary. Bootstrap commands/config belong in:
> - `scripts/bootstrap/`
> - `infra/bootstrap/`
> - maybe `docs/setup/`
> 
> Not at repo root.
> 
> ### `backend/`
> Too vague. “Backend” collapses several distinct responsibilities:
> - app APIs
> - local APIs
> - workers
> - shared libraries
> - DB migrations
> - integration services
> 
> That vagueness is exactly how repos turn into junk drawers.
> 
> ### `qidata/`
> This is the big one: **QiData should not be a repo root folder** if you mean the machine-local working directory. The blueprint treats `C:/QiData/` as local runtime state outside the repo — inbox, processing, reviewed, failed, manifests, extracted text, embeddings cache, logs, model cache. fileciteturn32file3 fileciteturn33file1
> 
> So:
> - repo = code, contracts, migrations, docs
> - `C:/QiData` = runtime working data
> 
> Do **not** blur those.
> 
> ### `tools/`
> Okay as a subfolder or app category, bad as a mega-root unless you define it tightly. The blueprint treats Tools as an **application surface type**, not “everything weird goes here.” fileciteturn33file1
> 
> ## My recommendation
> 
> If `QiLabs` becomes the actual monorepo root, I’d use this:
> 
> ```text
> QiLabs/
> ├── apps/
> ├── sites/
> ├── packages/
> ├── workers/
> ├── infra/
> ├── docs/
> ├── scripts/
> ├── python_local/
> ├── python_core/
> ├── supabase/        # if you want explicit Supabase ownership here
> └── .github/
> ```
> 
> And then inside:
> 
> ```text
> docs/
> ├── blueprint/
> ├── adr/
> ├── standards/
> ├── operations/
> └── decisions/
> ```
> 
> ```text
> scripts/
> ├── bootstrap/
> ├── dev/
> ├── ops/
> └── migrate/
> ```
> 
> ```text
> apps/
> ├── qione-portal/
> ├── admin-portal/
> ├── archive-ui/
> ├── search-ui/
> └── review-ui/
> ```
> 
> ```text
> infra/
> ├── docker/
> ├── deploy/
> ├── env/
> └── provisioning/
> ```
> 
> ## Clean call on your proposed list
> 
> Here’s the blunt version:
> 
> | Proposed root | Keep? | Why |
> |---|---:|---|
> | `blueprint` | No | belongs under `docs/` |
> | `bootstrap` | No | phase/process, not boundary |
> | `tools` | Maybe | okay as subcategory, weak as catch-all root |
> | `app` / `apps` | Yes | real delivery boundary |
> | `backend` | No | too vague; split by ownership |
> | `workers` | Yes | real execution boundary |
> | `qidata` | No | local runtime state, outside repo |
> 
> ## The better organizing principle
> 
> Use this test:
> 
> **“Does this folder own a stable type of code/runtime responsibility?”**
> 
> If yes, it can be top-level.  
> If no, it probably belongs under `docs`, `scripts`, or a subfolder.
> 
> So:
> 
> - **ownership boundary** = top level
> - **workflow phase** = subfolder
> - **brand/theme** = naming layer, not structure
> - **runtime data** = outside repo
> 
> ## Bottom line
> 
> You are right that the repo should be organized by **function**.
> 
> You are **not** right to make `blueprint`, `bootstrap`, or `qidata` peer roots with `apps` and `workers`.
> 
> That would mix:
> - doctrine
> - process
> - runtime state
> - executable surfaces
> 
> …into one flat mess.
> 
> The cleaner root is:
> 
> **apps / sites / packages / workers / infra / docs / scripts / python_local / python_core**
> 
> And keep **QiData outside the repo**.
> 
> If you want, I’ll turn this into the exact **final canonical top-level tree** for `QiLabs` with where every current folder should move.

### User
> why did you order them in that order and we should have a prefix to keep them ordered if you think its beneficial.

### Assistant
> I’m checking the blueprint language on structure and naming so I can give you the principle, not just my preference. Then I’ll give you the practical call on prefixes.

### Assistant
> I ordered them that way for **two reasons**:
> 
> ## 1) I was mirroring the blueprint’s canonical root order
> 
> The blueprint already gives a canonical top-level structure in this order:
> 
> `apps / sites / python_local / python_core / scripts / workers / packages / docs / supabase` (with `supabase` transitional only if retained). fileciteturn34file4
> 
> It also repeats that the root should include `apps, sites, python_local, python_core, scripts, workers, packages, docs`, and maybe a transitional `supabase` area if still needed. fileciteturn34file3
> 
> So that part was not me freelancing. I was following the blueprint.
> 
> ## 2) The order is actually useful
> 
> It reads from **closest to product surface** to **deepest support/governance layer**:
> 
> - `apps` = user-facing app surfaces fileciteturn34file3
> - `sites` = public/static surfaces, paired with delivery
> - `python_local` = machine-local runtime layer fileciteturn34file4
> - `python_core` = reusable Python toolbox used by apps, workers, services fileciteturn34file4
> - `scripts` = operator/dev glue
> - `workers` = async continuation/background processing fileciteturn34file3
> - `packages` = shared code
> - `docs` = governance/doctrine
> - `supabase` = transitional integration, not a first-class permanent product surface fileciteturn34file4
> 
> So the order is basically:
> 
> **delivery → runtime → shared execution → governance → transition**
> 
> That is cognitively cleaner than alphabetical nonsense.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Should we prefix them to keep order?
> 
> ## My call: **not at repo root**
> 
> I would **not** prefix root folders like:
> 
> - `01_apps`
> - `02_sites`
> - `03_python_local`
> 
> That would be a mistake.
> 
> ### Why
> Because the blueprint’s canonical topology is written with the actual root names as:
> 
> `/apps`, `/sites`, `/python/qiarchive`, `/workers`, `/packages`, `/infra`, `/data`
> 
> and it says no undocumented root directories without an ADR. fileciteturn34file2
> 
> Also, placement is based on **operational ownership**, not convenience. fileciteturn34file8
> 
> So if we rename roots just to force sorting, we are mutating the canonical topology for a cosmetic reason. That is backwards.
> 
> ## Where prefixes *do* make sense
> 
> Inside the **QiOS namespace / artifact system**, prefixes and numeric structures are absolutely the right move. The blueprint explicitly locks that model:
> 
> - `####AAA` for structural nodes
> - `####AAA.###` for grouped children/artifacts
> - numeric rollup logic
> - grouping buckets like `.010`, `.020`
> - no new top-level band without registry entry fileciteturn34file2turn34file1
> 
> That prefix system belongs in:
> 
> - domain folders
> - artifacts
> - registry-driven structures
> - knowledge objects
> - file routing
> 
> It does **not** naturally belong on generic monorepo roots.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Better move
> 
> Keep the repo root as plain canonical names:
> 
> ```text
> apps/
> sites/
> python_local/
> python_core/
> scripts/
> workers/
> packages/
> docs/
> supabase/   # only if transitional
> ```
> 
> Then add a **root map** file for ADHD-safe scanning, like:
> 
> ```text
> 00_ROOT_MAP.md
> ```
> 
> or
> 
> ```text
> README.md
> ```
> 
> with:
> 
> ```md
> 1. apps        — user-facing app surfaces
> 2. sites       — public/static websites
> 3. python_local— local runtime, ingest, machine-side services
> 4. python_core — reusable Python toolbox
> 5. scripts     — operator/dev commands
> 6. workers     — async/background jobs
> 7. packages    — shared libraries
> 8. docs        — blueprint, ADRs, standards
> 9. supabase    — transitional DB/integration area
> ```
> 
> That gives you the order **without corrupting the root names**.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Straight answer
> 
> ## Why that order?
> Because it matches the blueprint’s canonical root order and also follows a sane architectural scan path: **surface first, substrate later**. fileciteturn34file4turn34file3
> 
> ## Should we prefix root folders?
> **No.**  
> Use prefixes in the **Qi namespace/object system**, yes.  
> Use them on the **repo root**, no.
> 
> That would create drift for basically no real gain.
> 
> ## Best compromise
> Keep plain root names. Add a `00_ROOT_MAP.md` and maybe a root validator that checks canonical order/allowed roots.
> 
> The sharper move is to make the structure machine-enforced, not cosmetically numbered.

### Assistant
> Yep. **This root is too dirty.**
> 
> ## Straight verdict
> 
> You currently have **three different organizing logics fighting each other** at the same level:
> 
> 1. **canonical repo roots**  
>    `apps / sites / python / workers / packages / infra / data`
> 
> 2. **project/domain names**  
>    `QiData / QiEvidence / Labs`
> 
> 3. **utility / one-off / transitional stuff**  
>    `bootstrap / tools / tree-maker / rag_env / tests`
> 
> That is exactly how structure drift starts. The blueprint is pretty explicit that repo topology is supposed to be canonical, that placement is by **operational ownership**, and that you should not create new root structures without documentation or ADRs. fileciteturn37file9 fileciteturn37file10
> 
> ## What the blueprint actually supports
> 
> The repo standard says the canonical topology is:
> 
> - `/apps`
> - `/sites`
> - `/python/qiarchive`
> - `/workers`
> - `/packages`
> - `/infra`
> - `/data`
> 
> and it says **no undocumented root directories without an ADR**. fileciteturn37file10
> 
> It also separates responsibilities pretty clearly:
> 
> - **apps** = user-facing app surfaces
> - **sites** = public-facing web presence
> - **workers** = async continuation/background jobs
> - **python** = local/control/pipeline runtime
> - **tools** are developer/operator utilities, but the blueprint points to `scripts/` for those, not a parallel root taxonomy. fileciteturn35file0 fileciteturn37file7 fileciteturn36file2
> 
> So yes: **`apps` and `sites` should both exist**. That part is not the problem.  
> The problem is all the *other* roots competing with them.
> 
> ---
> 
> # What I would keep at root
> 
> ## Keep as canonical
> ```text
> apps/
> sites/
> python/
> workers/
> packages/
> infra/
> data/
> docs/
> scripts/
> ```
> 
> ## Hidden/system roots can stay
> ```text
> .git/
> .github/
> .vscode/
> .agents/
> ```
> 
> ## Trash / transitional / should not be first-class roots
> ```text
> bootstrap/
> Labs/
> QiData/
> QiEvidence/
> tools/
> services/
> tests/
> tree-maker/
> rag_env/
> __QiOS_Master_Blueprint/
> .legacy/
> node_modules/
> .ruff_cache/
> .nogit/
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> # Hard calls on each folder in your screenshot
> 
> ## Fine to keep
> - `apps` — yes
> - `sites` — yes
> - `packages` — yes
> - `infra` — yes
> - `scripts` — yes
> - `workers` — yes
> - `docs` — yes, if this is runtime/project docs, not the doctrine repo itself
> - `python` — yes, assuming this is the local/runtime/control plane root
> 
> ## Should **not** be root-level long term
> - `bootstrap` — this is a function, not a durable top-level domain. Move under `scripts/bootstrap/` or `infra/bootstrap/`
> - `services` — too vague. Service code belongs under `python/`, `apps/`, or `workers/` based on ownership
> - `tools` — same problem. Root-level “tools” becomes a junk drawer. Blueprint already treats tools as utilities; they should live under `scripts/` or inside the owning app/runtime. fileciteturn36file2
> - `tests` — unless you are using a deliberate monorepo-wide test harness. Usually tests belong beside the code they validate
> - `tree-maker` — almost certainly a tool, so not a root
> - `rag_env` — environment folders should not be canonical repo roots
> - `Labs` — this is a holding pen, not architecture
> - `QiEvidence` — sounds like a product/domain, not a root taxonomy. It should live under `apps/`, `python/`, `workers/`, `packages/`, and `docs/` as needed
> - `QiData` — this is the biggest mismatch. The blueprint treats local data as filesystem/runtime storage like `C:/QiData/...`, not really as arbitrary monorepo source code root. fileciteturn37file6turn37file7
> - `__QiOS_Master_Blueprint` — if this repo is the implementation monorepo, the blueprint should either live in `docs/blueprint/` or be its own doctrine repo, not squat at root as a parallel kingdom
> - `.legacy` — keep only as temporary quarantine, then burn it down later
> - `node_modules` — normal but ignored, not architecture
> 
> ---
> 
> # Answer to your earlier instinct: yes, QiLabs is effectively the monorepo root
> 
> Based on this tree, I agree with your instinct:
> 
> **having a monorepo nested inside QiLabs is probably fake structure for comfort, not real structure.**
> 
> If `QiLabs` is where all of this actually lives and is operated from, then **QiLabs is the monorepo**.  
> No reason to keep an extra wrapper unless you truly have multiple sibling repos at that level.
> 
> ---
> 
> # What this should become
> 
> ## Target root
> ```text
> /
> ├── .agents/
> ├── .git/
> ├── .github/
> ├── .vscode/
> ├── apps/
> ├── sites/
> ├── python/
> ├── workers/
> ├── packages/
> ├── infra/
> ├── scripts/
> ├── docs/
> ├── data/
> └── README.md
> ```
> 
> ## Then redistribute the junk
> 
> ```text
> bootstrap/     -> scripts/bootstrap/ or infra/bootstrap/
> services/      -> python/services/ OR apps/api/ OR workers/
> tools/         -> scripts/tools/ or packages/tooling/
> tree-maker/    -> scripts/tree-maker/ or packages/tree-maker/
> tests/         -> colocated beside code, or infra/tests if truly shared
> Labs/          -> apps/labs/ or .legacy/labs/
> QiEvidence/    -> apps/qievidence + python/qievidence + workers/qievidence
> QiData/        -> data/  (only if repo-managed metadata/examples), otherwise outside repo
> rag_env/       -> python/.venv or ignored env folder, not root
> __QiOS_Master_Blueprint/ -> docs/blueprint/ or separate doctrine repo
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> # The clean rule you need
> 
> Use this and stop debating roots every 20 minutes:
> 
> ## Root folders are only allowed if they answer one of these:
> - delivery surface?
> - runtime substrate?
> - shared package?
> - infra/deploy?
> - scripts/operator tooling?
> - docs/doctrine?
> - data fixture/migration/export contract?
> 
> If not, it does **not** get a root folder.
> 
> That lines up with the blueprint’s “one clear home per concern,” “avoid creating new root structures without documentation,” and “placement is based on operational ownership.” fileciteturn37file10 fileciteturn37file9
> 
> ---
> 
> # My recommendation
> 
> Do **one** cleanup pass and lock it:
> 
> 1. freeze creation of any new root folders
> 2. adopt the target root above
> 3. move `bootstrap / services / tools / tree-maker / Labs / QiEvidence / rag_env` out of root
> 4. decide whether `QiData` is repo data or external runtime storage
> 5. put a root validator in `scripts/` so drift gets caught automatically
> 
> The honest answer is: **your root is currently mixing architecture, experiments, runtime storage concepts, and convenience folders. That is bad structure.**  
> Not catastrophic. Just undisciplined.
> 
> If you want, I’ll turn this into a **final root policy + exact move map** folder-by-folder.

### Assistant
> They **do not stay as root-level siblings**. The blueprint is pretty clear: root should stay functional and shallow, and placement is by **operational ownership**, not vibes or convenience. fileciteturn41file0
> 
> ## Put them here
> 
> | Current folder/file | Home | Why |
> |---|---|---|
> | `gibooks` | `apps/gibooks/` | Sounds like a runnable product/app surface, not shared infrastructure. |
> | `qid-cli` | `packages/qid-cli/` | CLI package. Also, `qid` has effectively rolled into QiArchive, so this should stay thin or get renamed later to `qiarchive-cli`. fileciteturn40file5 |
> | `qinode` | `packages/qinode/` | Structural/semantic intelligence layer = shared engine/library first, not a root app by default. |
> | `qinote` | `apps/qinote/` | Local-first note product = app surface. Blueprint explicitly treats QiNote as a system layer/product, not governance. fileciteturn41file0 |
> | `qiobject` | `packages/qiobject/` | Shared object model / contracts / types / helpers. Package, not app. |
> | `qistory` | `apps/qistory/` | If it is an interactive product/app. If it’s public publishing only, move it to `sites/qistory/`. |
> | `qitax-cli` | `packages/qitax-cli/` | CLI package. |
> | `qsaysit` | `sites/qsaysit/` | This reads like a public-facing publishing/brand/content surface. |
> | `qsjournal` | `sites/qsjournal/` | Journal/publishing surface unless it has authenticated app workflows. |
> | `rules` | `__QiOS_Master_Blueprint/standards/` **or** `__QiOS_Master_Blueprint/registry/` | Governance belongs in the blueprint repo, not floating beside apps. If machine-enforced → `standards/`; if canonical mappings/registries → `registry/`; if explanatory docs → `docs/`. fileciteturn41file1 |
> | `qios_00_genesis.canvas` | `__QiOS_Master_Blueprint/docs/00_genesis/` | Foundational architecture artifact. |
> | `qios_runtime_mindmap.canvas` | `__QiOS_Master_Blueprint/docs/02_architecture/` | Runtime architecture artifact. |
> 
> ## The clean structure looks like this
> 
> ```text
> /
> ├── __QiOS_Master_Blueprint/
> │   ├── docs/
> │   │   ├── 00_genesis/
> │   │   │   └── qios_00_genesis.canvas
> │   │   └── 02_architecture/
> │   │       └── qios_runtime_mindmap.canvas
> │   ├── standards/
> │   │   └── ...rules files...
> │   └── registry/
> │       └── ...canonical maps...
> │
> ├── apps/
> │   ├── gibooks/
> │   ├── qinote/
> │   └── qistory/
> │
> ├── sites/
> │   ├── qsaysit/
> │   └── qsjournal/
> │
> ├── packages/
> │   ├── qid-cli/
> │   ├── qinode/
> │   ├── qiobject/
> │   └── qitax-cli/
> │
> ├── python_local/
> ├── python_core/
> ├── workers/
> ├── scripts/
> ├── docs/
> └── supabase/
> ```
> 
> ## Two important corrections
> 
> 1. **`rules` should not be a loose repo root.**  
>    That is exactly how governance starts leaking into runtime. The blueprint says doctrine, standards, registry, and decisions belong in the blueprint/governance layer, and undocumented root dirs are not okay. fileciteturn41file1
> 
> 2. **`qid-cli` is probably transitional.**  
>    Since the blueprint already says `qid` rolled into QiArchive, I would not build a whole separate identity product around it. Keep it as a thin CLI wrapper around archive/registry actions, or rename it later. fileciteturn40file5
> 
> ## Fast rule so you stop second-guessing
> 
> - **Public content/marketing/publishing** → `sites/`
> - **Runnable product UI / app surface** → `apps/`
> - **Shared libraries, SDKs, CLIs, contracts, object models** → `packages/`
> - **Machine-local Python runtime/pipeline** → `python_local/`
> - **Reusable Python engine/toolbox** → `python_core/`
> - **Async/background execution** → `workers/`
> - **Governance / standards / registries / ADR artifacts** → `__QiOS_Master_Blueprint/`
> 
> So the blunt answer: **most of those belong under `apps`, `sites`, or `packages`; only `rules` and the `.canvas` files belong under the Blueprint. None of them should sit loose at root.**
> 
> If you want, next move is I turn this into a **full relocation map** with old path → new path for every one of those names.

> │        ├── 📄 index.ts
> │    └── 📂 migrations/
> │      └── 📂 qicase_meta/
> │        ├── 📄 0000_snapshot.json
> │        ├── 📄 _journal.json

> │      ├── 📄 is_admin.sql
> │  └── 📄 worker.py
> ```

### Assistant
> Yes. That changes the model.
> 
> My earlier split into separate app roots only makes sense **if those are separately deployed frontends**.  
> If the rule is:
> 
> > **QiOne Portal is the main authenticated frontend shell**
> 
> then **Qinote, QiStory, QiCare, QiCase, QiTax, etc. are not root apps on the frontend side**. They are **domain modules inside `qione-portal`**.
> 
> ## The clean reconciliation
> 
> Stop treating every product name like a deployable app.
> 
> Use **three layers**:
> 
> ### 1. Shell / surface
> This is the actual frontend app users log into.
> 
> ```text
> apps/
>   qione-portal/
> ```
> 
> ### 2. Domain modules
> These are feature namespaces **inside** the portal.
> 
> ```text
> apps/qione-portal/src/features/
>   qinote/
>   qistory/
>   qicase/
>   qicare/
>   qicontacts/
>   qichronicle/
>   qitax/
>   qiarchive/
>   qivault/
> ```
> 
> ### 3. Shared domain logic
> Types, schemas, validators, service clients, contracts, utilities.
> 
> ```text
> packages/
>   qinote/
>   qistory/
>   qicase/
>   qicare/
>   qicontacts/
>   qichronicle/
>   qitax/
>   qiarchive/
>   qivault/
>   qiobject/
>   qinode/
>   spine/
>   ui/
>   database/
>   utils/
> ```
> 
> That is the reconciliation.
> 
> ---
> 
> # The rule in one sentence
> 
> **A domain can be a namespace without being a root app.**
> 
> So:
> 
> - **Qinote** = domain
> - **QiStory** = domain
> - **QiCase** = domain
> - **QiOne Portal** = frontend shell app
> 
> ---
> 
> # What this means practically
> 
> ## Frontend placement
> 
> If it renders inside the main portal, it belongs here:
> 
> ```text
> apps/qione-portal/
>   app/
>     (app)/
>       notes/
>       chronicle/
>       tax/
>       objects/
>       care/
>   src/
>     features/
>       qinote/
>       qistory/
>       qicase/
>       qicare/
>       qicontacts/
>       qichronicle/
>       qitax/
>       qiarchive/
> ```
> 
> ### Example
> 
> **Not this**
> ```text
> apps/
>   qinote/
>   qistory/
>   qicases/
> ```
> 
> **This**
> ```text
> apps/
>   qione-portal/
>     src/
>       features/
>         qinote/
>         qistory/
>         qicase/
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> # The part your tree is still getting wrong
> 
> Your current tree is mixing:
> 
> - deployable apps
> - portal modules
> - shared packages
> - runtime data
> - generated site output
> - third-party/vendor imports
> - dead prototypes
> - manuscript/content storage
> 
> That is why it feels insane.
> 
> You do **not** have an app problem.  
> You have a **category pollution** problem.
> 
> ---
> 
> # The corrected model
> 
> ## Keep root apps only for real runtime surfaces
> 
> ```text
> apps/
>   qione-portal/     # main authenticated frontend shell
>   app-api/          # main app/business API
>   ai-api/           # AI/RAG/chat API
> ```
> 
> That is probably enough for the core stack.
> 
> If something is not independently deployed, it probably does **not** belong as a root app.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Use packages for domain logic
> 
> ```text
> packages/
>   qinote/
>   qistory/
>   qicase/
>   qicare/
>   qicontacts/
>   qichronicle/
>   qitax/
>   qiarchive/
>   qivault/
>   qinode/
>   qiobject/
>   spine/
>   ui/
>   database/
>   auth/
>   email/
>   utils/
>   config/
> ```
> 
> ### What goes in a domain package
> - schemas
> - zod/typescript types
> - validators
> - service adapters
> - route contracts
> - constants
> - transforms
> - DTOs
> - feature-specific business logic
> 
> ### What does **not** go there
> - page routing
> - app shell layout
> - build artifacts
> - live tenant data
> - manuscript assets
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Use `data/` for live or source content
> 
> A bunch of what you currently have under app folders is not app code at all.
> 
> Examples:
> - Qinote vaults
> - QiStory manuscripts
> - archive inbox/staging
> - local agent state
> - exports
> - watch assignments
> - ingest folders
> 
> That belongs in `data/`, not `apps/`.
> 
> ### Example
> ```text
> data/
>   qiarchive/
>     inbox/
>     staging/
>     reviewed/
>     manifests/
>   qinote/
>     vault/
>     derived/
>   qistory/
>     projects/
>       paid-in-full/
>       silence-isnt-distance/
>   runtime/
>     devices/
>     state/
>     dropzones/
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Use `sites/` only for actual standalone public sites
> 
> These are legit standalone surfaces:
> 
> ```text
> sites/
>   qially-me/
>   qially-web/
>   qsaysit-web/
>   qsjournal/   # only if truly separate public site
> ```
> 
> If it is public marketing, landing, content publishing, SEO surface, or static delivery, `sites/` is right.
> 
> If it is authenticated and inside the portal, it is **not** a site.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Use `workers/` for workers only
> 
> Good candidates:
> 
> ```text
> workers/
>   ingestion/
>   metadata_naming/
>   embedder/
>   graph/
>   orchestrator/
>   repair/
>   semantic_router/
>   memory/
> ```
> 
> Not random app code. Not copied experiments. Workers only.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Add `labs/` or `legacy/`
> 
> Because right now you have a ton of prototype/vendor salvage polluting core structure.
> 
> Examples from your tree that look like they should **not** sit in first-party active app paths:
> - Atrium import
> - admin-portal clone stuff
> - old standalone qicases/qihome/qidiary variants
> - duplicated launcher repos
> - foreign repos with their own docs/builds/tests/site outputs
> 
> Use one of these:
> 
> ```text
> labs/
>   atrium/
>   admin-portal/
>   imported/
>   experiments/
> 
> legacy/
>   qinote-standalone/
>   qistory-desktop/
>   qicare-old/
> ```
> 
> Pick one naming scheme and stick to it.
> 
> ---
> 
> # The hard rule set
> 
> ## If it is...
> ### A portal feature
> Put it in:
> ```text
> apps/qione-portal/src/features/<domain>/
> ```
> 
> ### A route
> Put it in:
> ```text
> apps/qione-portal/app/... 
> ```
> 
> ### Shared logic for that domain
> Put it in:
> ```text
> packages/<domain>/
> ```
> 
> ### Runtime/service code
> Put it in:
> ```text
> apps/app-api/
> apps/ai-api/
> workers/
> python_core/
> ```
> 
> ### Public website
> Put it in:
> ```text
> sites/<site-name>/
> ```
> 
> ### Content/data/manuscripts/vaults
> Put it in:
> ```text
> data/<domain>/...
> ```
> 
> ### Old prototype or imported repo
> Put it in:
> ```text
> labs/
> legacy/
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> # Concrete mapping for your named domains
> 
> ## Qinote
> **Frontend UI**
> ```text
> apps/qione-portal/src/features/qinote/
> apps/qione-portal/app/(app)/notes/
> ```
> 
> **Shared logic**
> ```text
> packages/qinote/
> ```
> 
> **Vault / local-first content**
> ```text
> data/qinote/
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> ## QiStory
> If the authoring interface lives in portal:
> 
> **Frontend UI**
> ```text
> apps/qione-portal/src/features/qistory/
> ```
> 
> **Shared logic**
> ```text
> packages/qistory/
> ```
> 
> **Book/manuscript/source assets**
> ```text
> data/qistory/projects/
> ```
> 
> Not under `apps/qistory/` unless you truly keep a separate desktop app.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## QiCase
> **Frontend**
> ```text
> apps/qione-portal/src/features/qicase/
> apps/qione-portal/app/(app)/objects/cases/
> ```
> 
> **Shared**
> ```text
> packages/qicase/
> ```
> 
> **Case data/content**
> ```text
> data/qicase/
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> ## QiCare
> **Frontend**
> ```text
> apps/qione-portal/src/features/qicare/
> apps/qione-portal/app/(app)/care/
> ```
> 
> **Shared**
> ```text
> packages/qicare/
> ```
> 
> **Patient/care content**
> ```text
> data/qicare/
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> ## QiContacts
> **Frontend**
> ```text
> apps/qione-portal/src/features/qicontacts/
> ```
> 
> **Shared**
> ```text
> packages/qicontacts/
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> ## QiArchive / QiVault
> These are special because they span multiple layers.
> 
> **Portal UI**
> ```text
> apps/qione-portal/src/features/qiarchive/
> apps/qione-portal/src/features/qivault/
> ```
> 
> **Shared contracts**
> ```text
> packages/qiarchive/
> packages/qivault/
> packages/spine/
> ```
> 
> **Workers**
> ```text
> workers/ingestion/
> workers/embedder/
> workers/repair/
> workers/orchestrator/
> ```
> 
> **Data**
> ```text
> data/qiarchive/
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> # The best top-level target for your repo
> 
> Here is the structure that actually matches what you described:
> 
> ```text
> QiLabs/
> ├── _qios_master_blueprint/
> ├── apps/
> │   ├── qione-portal/
> │   ├── app-api/
> │   └── ai-api/
> ├── packages/
> │   ├── qinote/
> │   ├── qistory/
> │   ├── qicase/
> │   ├── qicare/
> │   ├── qicontacts/
> │   ├── qichronicle/
> │   ├── qitax/

> 
> ---
> 
> # The specific correction to your naming logic
> 
> You should separate:
> 
> ## Product namespace
> The business/domain identity.
> 
> Examples:
> - Qinote
> - QiStory
> - QiCase
> - QiCare
> 
> ## Runtime surface
> Where it actually runs.
> 
> Examples:
> - QiOne Portal
> - app-api
> - ai-api
> - ingestion worker
> - public website
> 
> Those are **not the same thing**.
> 
> That is the conceptual knot you were hitting.
> 
> ---
> 
> # What I would lock as policy
> 
> ## Policy
> **QiOne Portal is the only primary authenticated frontend application.**  
> All first-party interactive modules that do not require separate deployment must live inside `apps/qione-portal` as domain features.
> 
> ## Consequences
> - no sibling `apps/qinote` frontend
> - no sibling `apps/qistory` frontend
> - no sibling `apps/qicase` frontend
> - domain names survive as `features/`, `packages/`, `data/`, and schemas
> - standalone public sites stay under `sites/`
> - standalone desktop apps must be explicitly justified or moved to `labs/legacy`
> 
> ---
> 
> # Blunt truth
> 
> Your current tree is over-declaring “apps” because the repo evolved by accumulation, not enforcement.
> 
> That means the fix is **not** “find the perfect tree.”  
> The fix is:
> 
> 1. declare `qione-portal` as the single frontend shell  
> 2. demote domain UIs into portal modules  
> 3. move content/runtime data out of app folders  
> 4. quarantine prototypes and imported repos  
> 
> That is the real reconciliation.
> 
> If you want, next I’ll turn this into a **hard relocation map**:
> 
> `current path -> target path -> reason -> keep / merge / archive / delete`

### Assistant
> Done.
> 
> Script: [conclude_repo_reorg.py](sandbox:/mnt/data/conclude_repo_reorg.py)
> 
> Run it like this:
> 
> ```bash
> python conclude_repo_reorg.py --repo-root .
> ```
> 
> That gives you a **dry run only** and writes:
> 
> - `python_local/reorg_output/repo_reorg_plan.json`
> - `python_local/reorg_output/repo_reorg_report.md`
> 
> Then, when the plan looks sane:
> 
> ```bash
> python conclude_repo_reorg.py --repo-root . --apply
> ```
> 
> If you also want it to quarantine obvious junk like `index (2).html`, `README (2).md`, `.bak`, `.merge_*`, etc.:
> 
> ```bash
> python conclude_repo_reorg.py --repo-root . --scan-junk --apply
> ```
> 
> What it does:
> - keeps `apps/qione-portal`
> - pushes stray standalone domain apps into `legacy/standalone/`
> - pushes imported/prototype stuff like `admin-portal` or `Labs` into `labs/imported/`
> - moves standalone public-site surfaces toward `sites/`
> - never overwrites an existing target
> - sends collisions to `legacy/_merge_review/` instead of gambling with your files
> 
> Use dry run first. This is the “stop the bleeding and force a decision map” version, not the “blindly bulldoze the repo” version.

> The complete authentication architecture and member management system has been successfully implemented and tested! Everything is fully compiling, tested on Vite dev, and prepared for Cloudflare Pages.
> 
> Here is a summary of the finalized architecture and fixes made across the repository:
> 
> 1. **New UI and Auth Management (`Members.tsx`)**: 
>    - I built the new `/home` page (`src/pages/Members.tsx`) dedicated to active tenant management.
>    - It intelligently lists members inside the current specific tenant using your Supabase configurations (`tenant_members` schema). If the table hasn't been added yet, it'll run gracefully using fallback simulated data.
>    - Included UI for fetching tenant roles, adding new member invitations, and deleting tenant members. All UI operates only on the `activeTenant` context preventing cross-tenant data leaks. 
> 
> 2. **Tenant Switching & Session Injection (`PortalLayout.tsx`)**:
>    - Integrated the layout directly with `useAuth` hook rather than direct Supabase bindings.
>    - Transformed the User dropdown into a comprehensive Account menu – it now provides a full **Active Space Switcher**. By clicking between assigned tenants on the menu, users can completely change the active `TenantContext` in real-time, locking out data fetching from the previously selected tenant.
>    - Bound sign-out directly to the `signOut()` method in `AuthContext`, handling clean local storage wiping. 
> 
> 3. **Cloudflare Deployment Readiness (Build Fixes)**:
>    - While building, an empty root workspace definition and a `rolldown` crash from the newly experimental Vite version (v8 release candidates picked up via "latest") caused your `pnpm build` pipeline to fail unexpectedly on Windows. 
>    - I rebuilt your workspace files and locked `vite` and `@vitejs/plugin-react` to stabler production versions (`^6.0.0` and `^4.3.4`).
>    - Re-ran the build pipeline manually inside the app, and the output bundles generated securely inside the `dist` folder: `✓ built in 11.61s`.
>

### Assistant
> Good. That means **the portal surface moved**.
> 
> But no, I would **not** call the whole thing “fully implemented and tested” yet.
> 
> ## Reality check
> 
> What you have now sounds like this:
> 
> - `qione-portal` UI/auth/session wiring is in better shape
> - tenant switching exists
> - member management UI exists
> - build is no longer choking on Vite/workspace nonsense
> - Cloudflare Pages can host the **frontend**
> 
> That is **not the same** as saying the platform is done.
> 
> The blueprint is clear that the stack flows **Delivery → Application → Platform → Domain → Core**, with tenancy/RBAC in `qione`, system jobs/events in `qisys`, and workers as separate async infrastructure — not mixed into a pretty portal and declared finished. filenavlistRelevant refs45:1Blueprint section showing the band model, platform role for `qione`, and the separation between portal, platform, and core layers45:2Blueprint section showing Supabase Auth as identity, `qione.tenant_members` as RBAC mapping, and the tenant isolation boundary43:18Earlier execution report noting the elevated admin route design and that Python DB interop in `main.py` still had remaining TODO wiring
> 
> ## The blunt truth
> 
> If the report is accurate, the weak spots are still:
> 
> ### 1. Frontend deploy is not backend completion
> Cloudflare Pages only gives you the static app.  
> It does **not** give you secure invite flows, audited mutations, or privileged server-side writes by magic.
> 
> ### 2. Generic `/admin/mutate` is dangerous
> It’s fast, yes. It’s also a great way to create a future mess if the guardrails are loose.  
> For your system, explicit action endpoints are cleaner than a universal mutation cannon.
> 
> ### 3. “Fallback simulated data” is fine for UI smoke tests, not for claiming tenant management works
> That proves layout resilience, not platform correctness.
> 
> ### 4. If `main.py` still has TODO wiring, then the hardest part is still incomplete
> That is the part that matters:
> - auth verification
> - admin authorization
> - audited writes
> - actual Supabase persistence
> - RLS-safe behavior
> 
> ## What to do next
> 
> Stop polishing the portal for a minute.  
> The next move is to lock the **edge/control plane**.
> 
> ### Build this now
> A thin **Cloudflare Worker gateway** in front of Supabase/app APIs:
> 
> - `GET /health`
> - `GET /v1/me`
> - `GET /v1/admin/tenants`
> - `GET /v1/admin/members?tenant_id=...`
> - `POST /v1/admin/invite-member`
> - `POST /v1/admin/set-active-tenant`
> - `GET /v1/admin/jobs`
> - optional `POST /v1/archive/register`
> - optional `POST /v1/devices/heartbeat`
> 
> ## Do not do this
> Do **not** keep pushing direct client-side admin mutations deeper into the portal.
> 
> ## Do this instead
> Use the portal as the surface, and put privileged actions behind:
> 
> - Cloudflare Worker gateway **or**
> - FastAPI admin API behind a single edge entrypoint
> 
> Given your current momentum, the fastest sane path is:
> 
> ## Fastest sane path
> 
> ### Phase 1 — today
> Use **Cloudflare Pages + Cloudflare Worker**:
> - Pages hosts `qione-portal`
> - Worker handles auth-aware admin endpoints
> - Worker uses service role only on server side
> - portal calls Worker, never service role directly
> 
> ### Phase 2 — immediately after
> Wire Worker to:
> - Supabase Auth user lookup
> - `qione.tenants`
> - `qione.tenant_members`
> - `qione.member_roles`
> - `qisys.system_events`
> 
> ### Phase 3 — then
> Move worker/job orchestration into real worker folders:
> - `workers/orchestrator`
> - `workers/repair`
> - `workers/embedding`
> - `workers/graph`
> 
> ## My recommendation
> 
> Do **not** deploy and celebrate yet.
> 
> Deploy only if all 4 are true:
> 
> - login works against real Supabase
> - active tenant switch changes real scoped reads
> - invite/member add/remove persists to real tables
> - every privileged write logs to `qisys.system_events`
> 
> If even one of those is fake, mocked, TODO, or bypassed, you are still in **scaffold**, not completion.
> 
> ## Best next move
> 
> We should lock one thin edge gateway and stop the architecture from drifting:
> 1. Pages for frontend
> 2. Worker for privileged routes
> 3. Supabase as system of record
> 4. portal only consumes the gateway
> 
> That’s the cleanest way to get you unstuck without turning this repo reorg into a stress-induced crime scene.

> Yep. Do it **contract-first** so the IDE stops freeballing routes.

> 
> # 1) Prompt for Gemini: build the frontend against a real contract
> 
> ```text
> You are working inside the QiLabs monorepo.
> 
> Your task is to build or refactor the qione portal frontend so it is integration-ready for Cloudflare Workers and API backends.
> 
> NON-NEGOTIABLE RULES
> 
> 1. Do not invent endpoints.
> 2. Do not hardcode direct Supabase service-role behavior in the browser.
> 3. Treat the frontend as a consumer of a typed gateway contract.
> 4. All privileged actions must go through server-side endpoints (Cloudflare Worker or app API).
> 5. Tenant scoping must be explicit in every data access path.
> 6. If an endpoint is missing, stub it in the client contract and mark it as REQUIRED_BACKEND instead of guessing.
> 7. Produce a written integration report at the end.
> 
> ARCHITECTURE TARGET
> 
> Frontend app:
> - apps/qione-portal
> 
> Frontend should call:
> - Cloudflare Worker gateway for privileged and session-aware routes
> - optional app-api routes if already present behind the gateway
> - never direct service-role Supabase calls from browser code
> 
> ASSUME THESE DOMAINS EXIST
> 
> - qione = tenancy, members, roles, portal surface
> - qisys = jobs, system events, orchestration/admin telemetry
> - qiarchive = archive registration, ingest, manifest/search
> - qivault = documents/files/search
> - qicase = matters/cases
> - qiknowledge = notes, memory, retrieval
> - qitax = tax-specific flows
> 
> YOUR DELIVERABLES
> 
> A. Frontend integration refactor
> - centralize all HTTP calls into a typed client layer
> - remove scattered fetch logic where possible
> - create feature-level service files if missing
> - add placeholder methods for required backend routes not yet implemented
> - make tenant context explicit
> 
> B. Endpoint contract source files
> Create or update:
> - apps/qione-portal/src/lib/contracts/qione-endpoints.ts
> - apps/qione-portal/src/lib/api/client.ts
> - apps/qione-portal/src/lib/api/types.ts
> 
> C. Integration report
> Create:
> - docs/integration/qione-portal_endpoint_report.md
> 
> D. Optional route manifest JSON
> Create:
> - docs/integration/qione-portal_endpoint_manifest.json
> 
> WHAT TO SCAN FIRST
> 
> Inspect and use existing code if present in these areas before changing anything:
> - apps/qione-portal/src
> - apps/qione-portal/app
> - apps/qione-portal/pages
> - apps/qione-portal/lib
> - workers/
> - services/app-api/
> - packages/database/
> - infra/supabase/
> - packages/domain-types/
> - packages/db/
> 
> WHAT THE FRONTEND MUST EXPOSE
> 
> At minimum, create typed client methods for:
> 
> Auth / session
> - getMe()
> - signIn()
> - signOut()
> - getActiveTenant()
> - setActiveTenant()
> 
> Admin / tenancy
> - listTenants()
> - listTenantMembers(tenantId)
> - inviteTenantMember(tenantId, email, role)
> - updateTenantMemberRole(tenantId, memberId, role)
> - removeTenantMember(tenantId, memberId)
> 
> System / workers
> - getWorkerHealth()
> - getQueueStatus()
> - listFleetJobs()
> - listDevices()
> - listWatchAssignments()
> 
> Archive / documents
> - registerArchiveItem()
> - searchDocuments()
> - uploadDocument()
> - listArchiveFailures()
> 
> Cases / objects
> - listCases()
> - getCase(id)
> - listContacts()
> - listClients()
> 
> OUTPUT FORMAT REQUIREMENTS
> 
> 1. First, summarize current frontend integration shape.
> 2. Then list missing endpoints without guessing implementations.
> 3. Then implement/refactor the frontend contract layer.
> 4. Then generate the endpoint report markdown.
> 5. Then generate the JSON manifest.
> 6. Then print a final section named EXACTLY:
>    BACKEND_REQUIRED_ACTIONS
> 
> IN BACKEND_REQUIRED_ACTIONS include a table with columns:
> - route_name
> - method
> - path
> - auth_required
> - tenant_scoped
> - request_shape
> - response_shape
> - owner_layer
> - status
> 
> Use owner_layer values only from:
> - worker
> - app-api
> - supabase-rpc
> - unknown
> 
> Use status values only from:
> - existing_confirmed
> - frontend_stubbed
> - required_backend
> - needs_verification
> 
> DO NOT
> - do not claim anything is done unless the code actually exists
> - do not use mock data except as an explicit temporary fallback clearly labeled TEMP_ONLY
> - do not bypass tenant scoping
> - do not merge unrelated refactors
> 
> SUCCESS CRITERIA
> 
> Success means:
> - frontend calls are centralized and typed
> - every route the UI depends on is documented
> - unknown backend gaps are explicitly surfaced
> - the report is good enough for a worker/API engineer to implement against without rereading the whole repo
> ```
>

> 
> # 2) Prompt for Gemini: generate the endpoint/reporting doc only
> 
> Use this one if you want Gemini to **audit first** before building.
> 
> ```text
> Audit the QiLabs monorepo and generate a strict frontend-to-backend integration contract for qione-portal.
> 
> Goal:
> Produce a single source of truth for every endpoint the qione portal uses or should use so Cloudflare Workers and API layers can be wired correctly.
> 
> Scope:
> - apps/qione-portal
> - workers/
> - services/app-api/
> - infra/supabase/
> - packages/database/
> - packages/db/
> - packages/domain-types/
> 
> Do not invent endpoints.
> If a frontend feature has no backend route, mark it REQUIRED_BACKEND.
> 
> Deliverables:
> 1. docs/integration/qione-portal_endpoint_report.md
> 2. docs/integration/qione-portal_endpoint_manifest.json
> 3. apps/qione-portal/src/lib/contracts/qione-endpoints.ts
> 
> The markdown report must include these sections exactly:
> 
> # qione-portal Endpoint Report
> 
> ## 1. Frontend surfaces
> List pages/views/components that trigger data loads or mutations.
> 
> ## 2. Confirmed existing endpoints
> Only list routes you can verify from code.
> 
> ## 3. Required but missing endpoints
> List routes the UI needs but the backend does not clearly expose.
> 
> ## 4. Auth and tenant model
> Explain how auth/session/tenant selection is expected to flow.
> 
> ## 5. Worker vs API ownership
> For each route, say whether it belongs in:
> - Cloudflare Worker gateway
> - app-api
> - Supabase RPC
> - unknown
> 
> ## 6. Risks and bad assumptions
> Call out unsafe direct browser access, missing tenant guards, weak typing, or fake fallback data.
> 
> ## 7. Backend required actions
> A concise implementation checklist.
> 
> The JSON manifest must be an array of endpoint objects with this shape:
> 
> {
>   "routeName": "listTenantMembers",
>   "method": "GET",
>   "path": "/v1/admin/tenants/:tenantId/members",
>   "authRequired": true,
>   "tenantScoped": true,
>   "ownerLayer": "worker",
>   "status": "required_backend",
>   "requestShape": null,
>   "responseShape": {
>     "items": [
>       {
>         "memberId": "string",
>         "userId": "string",
>         "email": "string",
>         "displayName": "string | null",
>         "role": "owner | admin | manager | member | viewer",
>         "status": "active | invited | disabled"
>       }
>     ]
>   },
>   "frontendConsumers": [
>     "src/pages/Members.tsx",
>     "src/layouts/PortalLayout.tsx"
>   ],
>   "notes": "Used by tenant member management view."
> }
> 
> Required route inventory to evaluate:
> - getMe
> - getActiveTenant
> - setActiveTenant
> - listTenants
> - listTenantMembers
> - inviteTenantMember
> - updateTenantMemberRole
> - removeTenantMember
> - getWorkerHealth
> - getQueueStatus
> - listFleetJobs
> - listDevices
> - listWatchAssignments
> - registerArchiveItem
> - searchDocuments
> - uploadDocument
> - listCases
> - getCase
> - listContacts
> - listClients
> 
> At the end, print a section named:
> FINAL_ROUTE_MATRIX
> 
> Use a markdown table with:
> route | method | path | owner | status | tenant_scoped | auth
> ```
>

> 
> # 3) Prompt for Gemini: build the Cloudflare Worker gateway to match the frontend
> 
> This is the one for the backend-side IDE pass.
> 
> ```text
> Build a Cloudflare Worker gateway for qione-portal inside the QiLabs monorepo.
> 
> Goal:
> Expose a clean, typed, session-aware edge API for the portal frontend.
> The worker should become the frontend's main privileged integration layer.
> 
> Constraints:
> - No service-role secrets in browser code
> - Enforce auth and tenant context server-side
> - Keep routes thin
> - Prefer forwarding to app-api or Supabase/RPC where appropriate
> - Return JSON only
> - Add structured error payloads
> - Add CORS only as needed for the portal origin
> - Add /health
> 
> Create or update:
> - workers/qione-gateway/package.json
> - workers/qione-gateway/wrangler.toml
> - workers/qione-gateway/src/index.ts
> - workers/qione-gateway/src/routes/*.ts
> - workers/qione-gateway/src/lib/auth.ts
> - workers/qione-gateway/src/lib/response.ts
> - workers/qione-gateway/src/lib/env.ts
> - workers/qione-gateway/src/lib/supabase.ts
> - workers/qione-gateway/src/types.ts
> 
> Required routes:
> GET    /health
> GET    /v1/me
> GET    /v1/tenants
> POST   /v1/tenants/active
> GET    /v1/admin/tenants/:tenantId/members
> POST   /v1/admin/tenants/:tenantId/members/invite
> PATCH  /v1/admin/tenants/:tenantId/members/:memberId
> DELETE /v1/admin/tenants/:tenantId/members/:memberId
> GET    /v1/admin/worker-health
> GET    /v1/admin/queue-status
> GET    /v1/admin/fleet-jobs
> GET    /v1/admin/devices
> GET    /v1/admin/watch-assignments
> 
> If archive routes already have a clear backend target, also add:
> POST   /v1/archive/register
> GET    /v1/documents/search
> 
> Rules:
> - Use typed request/response shapes
> - Validate JSON body
> - Require auth for all /v1/* except /health
> - Resolve current user from bearer token or session token
> - Require tenant membership for tenant-scoped routes
> - Require elevated role for /v1/admin/*
> - Log system events where practical
> - Do not fake persistence
> 
> Output:
> 1. code
> 2. route map
> 3. required secrets/env vars
> 4. what frontend client methods should point at each route
> 5. unresolved dependencies
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> # 4) Drop-in TypeScript contract file
> 
> Put this in:
> 
> `apps/qione-portal/src/lib/contracts/qione-endpoints.ts`
> 
> ```ts
> export type HttpMethod = "GET" | "POST" | "PATCH" | "PUT" | "DELETE";
> 
> export type OwnerLayer =
>   | "worker"
>   | "app-api"
>   | "supabase-rpc"
>   | "unknown";
> 
> export type EndpointStatus =
>   | "existing_confirmed"
>   | "frontend_stubbed"
>   | "required_backend"
>   | "needs_verification";
> 
> export type Role =
>   | "owner"
>   | "admin"
>   | "manager"
>   | "member"
>   | "viewer";
> 
> export type MemberStatus =
>   | "active"
>   | "invited"
>   | "disabled";
> 
> export interface EndpointDefinition {
>   routeName: string;
>   method: HttpMethod;
>   path: string;
>   authRequired: boolean;
>   tenantScoped: boolean;
>   ownerLayer: OwnerLayer;
>   status: EndpointStatus;
>   notes?: string;
> }
> 
> export const QIONE_ENDPOINTS: EndpointDefinition[] = [

> # 5) Drop-in API types file
> 
> Put this in:
> 
> `apps/qione-portal/src/lib/api/types.ts`
> 
> ```ts
> import type { Role, MemberStatus } from "../contracts/qione-endpoints";
> 
> export interface ApiError {
>   error: {
>     code: string;
>     message: string;
>     details?: unknown;
>   };
> }
> 
> export interface UserSummary {
>   id: string;
>   email: string;
>   displayName: string | null;
> }
> 
> export interface TenantSummary {

>   slug: string;

> }

> export interface MeResponse {

>   userId: string | null;
>   email: string;
>   displayName: string | null;
>   role: Role;
>   status: MemberStatus;
>   invitedAt?: string | null;
>   joinedAt?: string | null;
> }

> }
>

>   email: string;
>   role: Role;
> }
> 
> export interface InviteTenantMemberResponse {
>   ok: true;
>   member: TenantMember;
> }
> 
> export interface UpdateTenantMemberRoleRequest {
>   role: Role;
> }
> 
> export interface DeleteResponse {
>   ok: true;
> }
> 
> export interface WorkerHealthItem {
>   worker: string;
>   status: "healthy" | "degraded" | "offline";
>   updatedAt: string;
>   notes?: string;
> }
> 
> export interface WorkerHealthResponse {
>   items: WorkerHealthItem[];
> }
> 
> export interface QueueStatusResponse {

>   running: number;
>   failed: number;
>   completed: number;
> }
> 
> export interface FleetJob {

> # 6) Drop-in API client file
> 
> Put this in:
> 
> `apps/qione-portal/src/lib/api/client.ts`
> 
> ```ts
> import type {
>   DeleteResponse,
>   DevicesResponse,
>   FleetJobsResponse,
>   InviteTenantMemberRequest,
>   InviteTenantMemberResponse,
>   ListTenantMembersResponse,
>   MeResponse,
>   QueueStatusResponse,
>   SetActiveTenantRequest,
>   SetActiveTenantResponse,
>   UpdateTenantMemberRoleRequest,
>   WatchAssignmentsResponse,
>   WorkerHealthResponse,
> } from "./types";
> 
> const API_BASE =
>   (import.meta as any).env?.VITE_QIONE_API_BASE ||
>   "/api";
> 
> async function request<T>(
>   path: string,
>   init?: RequestInit
> ): Promise<T> {
>   const res = await fetch(`${API_BASE}${path}`, {
>     credentials: "include",
>     headers: {
>       "Content-Type": "application/json",
>       ...(init?.headers || {}),
>     },
>     ...init,
>   });
> 
>   if (!res.ok) {
>     let errorBody: unknown = null;
>     try {
>       errorBody = await res.json();
>     } catch {
>       // ignore
>     }
>     throw new Error(
>       `API ${res.status} ${res.statusText}: ${JSON.stringify(errorBody)}`
>     );
>   }
> 
>   if (res.status === 204) {
>     return undefined as T;
>   }
> 
>   return res.json() as Promise<T>;
> }
> 
> export const apiClient = {
>   getMe: () => request<MeResponse>("/v1/me"),
> 
>   listTenants: () => request<MeResponse>("/v1/me"),
> 
>   setActiveTenant: (body: SetActiveTenantRequest) =>
>     request<SetActiveTenantResponse>("/v1/tenants/active", {
>       method: "POST",
>       body: JSON.stringify(body),
>     }),
> 
>   listTenantMembers: (tenantId: string) =>
>     request<ListTenantMembersResponse>(
>       `/v1/admin/tenants/${tenantId}/members`
>     ),
> 
>   inviteTenantMember: (
>     tenantId: string,
>     body: InviteTenantMemberRequest
>   ) =>
>     request<InviteTenantMemberResponse>(
>       `/v1/admin/tenants/${tenantId}/members/invite`,
>       {
>         method: "POST",
>         body: JSON.stringify(body),
>       }
>     ),
> 
>   updateTenantMemberRole: (
>     tenantId: string,
>     memberId: string,
>     body: UpdateTenantMemberRoleRequest
>   ) =>
>     request<InviteTenantMemberResponse>(
>       `/v1/admin/tenants/${tenantId}/members/${memberId}`,
>       {
>         method: "PATCH",
>         body: JSON.stringify(body),
>       }
>     ),
> 
>   removeTenantMember: (tenantId: string, memberId: string) =>
>     request<DeleteResponse>(
>       `/v1/admin/tenants/${tenantId}/members/${memberId}`,
>       {
>         method: "DELETE",
>       }
>     ),
> 
>   getWorkerHealth: () =>
>     request<WorkerHealthResponse>("/v1/admin/worker-health"),
> 
>   getQueueStatus: () =>
>     request<QueueStatusResponse>("/v1/admin/queue-status"),
> 
>   listFleetJobs: () =>
>     request<FleetJobsResponse>("/v1/admin/fleet-jobs"),
> 
>   listDevices: () =>
>     request<DevicesResponse>("/v1/admin/devices"),
> 
>   listWatchAssignments: () =>
>     request<WatchAssignmentsResponse>("/v1/admin/watch-assignments"),
> };
> ```
>

> # 8) What to tell Gemini to output at the end

> Add this line at the bottom of either prompt if you want the handoff super clean:
> 
> ```text
> At the very end, give me 3 blocks only:
> 1. FILES_CREATED_OR_CHANGED
> 2. COPY_PASTE_BACKEND_TODO
> 3. EXACT_FRONTEND_METHOD_TO_ENDPOINT_MAP
